title: Wrist trauma: X-ray interpretation
secondary_keywords: wrist x ray interpretation orthopedic; how to read wrist trauma x ray; distal radius x ray interpretation; wrist fracture radiograph interpretation; wrist trauma imaging approach
all_keywords: wrist trauma x ray interpretation; wrist x ray interpretation orthopedic; how to read wrist trauma x ray; distal radius x ray interpretation; wrist fracture radiograph interpretation; wrist trauma imaging approach

The extensor carpi ulnaris groove (ECUG) visible radially next to the ulnar styloid. Distal radius and ulna, carpal bones and proximal metacarpals in view. Radiocarpal joint (between radius and lunate&scaphoid), distal radioulnar and...
source_knowledge:
Projections
PA projectionThe patient is seated with upper arm raised laterally, elbow flexed 90 degrees, and forearm in neutral pro-supination with palm flat on the detector. If humerus is not abducted, the hand will be pronated and the radius will appear shortened in relation to ulna.


In true PA image the radial and ulnar styloid processes appear in profile. The extensor carpi ulnaris groove (ECUG) visible radially next to the ulnar styloid.
Distal radius and ulna, carpal bones and proximal metacarpals in view. Radiocarpal joint (between radius and lunate&scaphoid), distal radioulnar and scapholunate joint in view.


Lateral projectionThe patient is seated with the elbow flexed 90 degrees, and the ulnar side of the hand placed against the detector with thumb pointing upward, ensuring the distal radius and ulna are superimposed.

Radius and ulna overlap distally in a true lateral projection. Another confirmation of a true lateral view is the pisiform's palmar cortex positioned between the anterior surfaces of the distal scaphoid and capitate.
The collinear alignment of radius, lunate and capitate in view. Distal part of the scaphoid (overlapping with pisiform) are seen volarly. The dorsal part of triquetrum and the tubercle of Lister on radius are seen on the dorsal side.


Oblique projection 
The scaphoid is seen in additional view with less overlapping with other carpal bones. The scaphoid tubercle is visible.


Ulnar deviation projection 

Ulnar deviation brings scaphoid "up" from scaphoid fossa on the radius and positions it more longitudinally. The proximal pole, distal pole and the waist of scaphoid are seen.

Structured evaluation of wrist trauma X-ray

General

Patient Identification and Date: Is the image most recent? Are there previous images available for comparison?

Area Verification: Does the X-ray capture the area of pain or suspected trauma?

Sufficient Projections: Multiple views are essential to provide a comprehensive assessment of the anatomy, confirm findings, and detect abnormalities that may be missed in a single view.

Are there fractures?

Look for cortex breaks, irregularities in bone cortex or density difference in bone structure. Displacement? Articular surface gaps/step offs?

Systematically examine relevant locations/anatomy:

Distal radius

Metaphysis

The normal metaphyseal region of the wrist shows continuous cortical bone in both PA and lateral projections. The trabecular bone shows normal heterogeneous pattern without focal disruptions. A thin physeal scar may be visible as a subtle sclerotic line in adults where the growth plate has ossified.

The lateral view shows normal slight volar tilt of the radial articular surface and the projection of Lister's tubercle on the dorsal side of radial metaphysis.

Metaphyseal fracture with dorsal angulation (Colles' fracture)

An extra-articular metaphyseal fracture with dorsal angulation of the distal fracture fragment. It commonly occurs from a fall on an outstretched hand, requiring moderate to high-energy impact in normal bone but can result from low-energy falls in osteoporotic bone.

Elderly, fall onto an outstretched hand. Minimally displaced fracture through the distal right radial metaphyseal region with dorsal angulation of the distal fracture component.

Metaphyseal fracture with volar angulation (Smith's or reverse Colles' fracture)

An extra-articular metaphyseal fracture with volar angulation of the distal fracture fragment. It typically results from a fall onto the back of the hand.

Elderly, fall onto a flexed wrist. An extra-articular transverse fracture of the distal radius with volar angulation. 

Articular surface and radial styloid

The normal radial articular surface shows a concave outline with defined margins (dorsal and volar rim) without gaps or step-offs. The articular suface consists of lunate fossa (yellow) and scaphoid fossa (green). The dorsal rim projects slightly more distally than the volar rim. 

On a PA view of the normal wrist, the articular surface slopes downward from the radial styloid toward the ulnar side (radial inclination).

The lateral view shows normal volar tilt of the radial articular surface. The styloid process of the radius overlaps with scaphoid and lunate bones.

Dorsal rim fracture (Dorsal Barton's fracture)

An intra-articular fracture through the dorsal rim, where the fracture fragment slides proximally rather than tilting dorsally (vs Colles).

Adult, history of fall with injury to right wrist. Distal radius fracture with fracture extending to intra-articular surface along dorsal aspect. Also, fracture of ulnar styloid process.

Volar rim fracture with carpal displacement (Volar Barton's fracture)

An intra-articular fracture through the volar rim of the distal radius with volar displacement of both the fragment and attached carpus as a unit.

Elderly, fall on outstreched hand. Intra-articular fracture through the palmar aspect of the distal radius with dorsal subluxation of the radius and ulna with respect to the carpus. Also ulnar styloid fracture.

Lunate fossa depression fracture (Die punch fracture)



An intra-articular impaction fracture involving depression of the lunate fossa of the distal radius, resulting from axial loading forces transmitted through the lunate bone.

Adult, fall from 2 meters. Comminuted intra-articular fracture extending through the radial styloid with deformity of the lunate facet.

Radial styloid fracture (Chauffeur's fracture)

An intra-articular fracture where the fracture line extends proximally in a variable direction from the radial articular surface through the lateral cortex.

Adult, direct impact to radial styloid. undisplaced inrta-articular fracture of the radial styloid process. 

Comminuted intra-articular fracture



Multiple fracture fragments involving both the articular surface and metaphysis, with disruption of joint congruity, often showing displacement, articular step-offs, and variable fracture patterns.

Adult, fall from 1.5m onto left out-stretched hand. Displaced, comminuted fracture of the right distal radius with associated fracture of ulnar styloid.

Diaphysis

About 1/3 of the distal radius diaphysis is visible in PA and lateral projections. The cortical outline should appear continuous without disruption. The sigmoid notch area, where the radius forms the distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ) with the ulnar head, should be assessed for alignment, as fractures of the diaphysis may be associated with DRUJ injuries.

In the lateral projection, the radius and ulna should overlap each other distally, provided that the image is true lateral (the pisiform palmar cortex is between the anterior surfaces of the distal scaphoid and capitate).

Forearm fracture

A fracture involving the diaphysis of the radius and/or ulna, may occur with variable patterns. These injuries typically result from direct trauma to the forearm, falls on an outstretched hand, or forceful twisting motions.

Adult, motorcycle accident. Transverse and displaced fracture of the distal left radius. Comminuted fracture of the left ulna with distal left ulna - carpal dislocation.

Radial shaft fracture with DRUJ disruption (Galeazzi fracture)

A fracture of the distal third of the radial shaft with widening of the DRUJ on AP image. It typically results from a fall on an outstretched hand, requiring high-energy trauma in most cases, or a direct trauma.

Adult, fall on outstreched hand. Transverse fracture of the mid-distal radial shaft. The DRUJ is widened.

Distal ulna

Diaphysis and ulnar styloid process

Examine the cortex of the ulnar diaphysis and ulnar styloid process on PA and lateral image. Fractures isolated to the diaphysis of the ulna are rare, whereas fractures of the ulnar styloid process are commonly seen in conjunction with distal radius fractures.

Ulna overlaps with radius in the lateral projection.

Ulnar shaft fracture

A disruption in the cortical continuity of ulnar diaphysis, which may appear as transverse, oblique, spiral, or comminuted depending on the mechanism. It commonly results from direct trauma such as a defensive injury from blocking a blow to the forearm.

Adult, trauma to right forearm after a fall. A fracture in the distal third of the right ulna with no significant displacement.

Ulnar styloid fracture

Appears as a displaced or non-displaced disruption of the styloid process at the tip or base. It is frequently seen as an associated injury with distal radius metaphyseal fractures due to the shared mechanism of injury and transmission of forces through the triangular fibrocartilage complex.

Adult, trauma, a distal radial fracture where there is an additional ulnar styloid fracture.

Carpal bones

Scaphoid

Assess the scaphoid's cortex and possible density differences. On a PA image, it appears boat-shaped with its proximal pole articulating with the radius. 

Scaphoid fractures, the second most common upper extremity fracture after distal radius fractures, may not always be visible on initial radiographs but become apparent on follow-up X-rays. CT or MRI are also used to diagnose scaphoid related pathology.

In the lateral view, the scaphoid appears quadrilateral with a distinct volar tilt orientation. It overlaps proximally with other carpal bones and distally with pisiform.

Oblique view of the normal scaphoid shows additional projection of scaphoid with minimal overlap. The scaphoid tubercle is visible distally. 

In ulnar deviation, the scaphoid rises from the scaphoid fossa and rotates to a more vertical orientation. This enhances the view of the scaphoid waist, the most fracture-prone area, and the proximal pole. 

Scaphoid waist fracture

Common fracture. Radiographically seen as a fracture line through the central portion (waist) of the scaphoid. Commonly caused by hyperextension of the wrist during a fall on an outstretched hand. 

Adult, fall. The transverse fracture of scaphoid is seen in ulnar deviation projection.

Scaphoid tubercle fracture

Tubercle fractures are avulsion fractures and part of distal pole scaphoid fractures. The tubercle is best seen in oblique view.

Adult, fall. Slightly displaced fracture of the distal scaphoid tubercle/pole.

Proximal pole scaphoid fracture

Represent about one-fifth of scaphoid fracture cases. Important to identify because of higher risk of avascular necrosis and nonunion due to the scaphoid's retrograde blood supply pattern, where vessels primarily enter distally and supply the proximal pole secondarily. The more proximal the fracture location, the poorer the healing potential.

Triquetrum and pisiform

Triquetrum overlaps with pisiform on the PA view but the cortices of both bones can usually still be assessed. Triquetrum is the second most common carpal bone to fracture. 

On the lateral view the outline of the triquetrum is difficult to define due to overlapping carpal bones, but its dorsal part is typically visible, which is a common fracture site. 

Pisiform can be seen in lateral image overlapping with the distal scaphoid.

Triquetral dorsal cortical fracture

Fairly common injury. Can be caused by avulsion or impaction. It can typically be seen as a dorsal bone flake on the lateral image, whereas the PA projection may appear normal.

Adult, sports injury. There is a small bone fragment at the dorsum of the triquetrum seen on the lateral view. The pisiform bone the triquetrum in the AP view and obscure the fracture.

Triquetral body fracture

A triquetral body fracture can be isolated, but is often associated with other carpal fractures or ligamentous injuries.

Adult, sports injury. Displaced fractures of the triquetrum and the ulnar styloid process. Also, perilunate dislocation.

Pisiform fracture

Uncommon fracture that typically result from fall on outstreched hand or direct blow. Can be isolated or associated with other bony injuries of the wrist area. Can be hard to identify from traditional x rays.

Adult, fall on ground. Faint lucency is noted in pisiform.

Hamate, capitate and lunate

The hamate, capitate, and lunate are traceable on PA view, where each should show continuous cortical margins without step-offs. The normal hook of the hamate can sometimes be seen on PA image as an oval sclerotic ring.

On a lateral view, the hamate and capitate overlap (the hook of the hamate might be visible volarly).

The capitate should be aligned with the cup shaped lunate and follow the alignment of the radius. Lunate dislocation is discussed below, fractures of lunate are rare.

Hamate body fracture

Uncommon fracture that is typically related to higher energy trauma. CT imaging is often needed to diagnose.

Trauma, fall down on right hand. Fracture is noted at the distal medial corner of the hamate bone.

Hook of hamate fracture

More common of hamate fractures. Typically result from acute or repetitive trauma in racquet or club sports where the handle impacts the hook. Often appearing as a disruption or complete separation of the process from the hamate body, often requiring additional imaging (CT) to diagnose.

Adult, fall onto golf club handle. Fracture through the hook of the hamate with ~2mm of diastasis between the fracture fragments, seen in lateral image.

Capitate fracture

Uncommon. Typically results from high-energy axial loading through the third metacarpal or from a fall with the force transmitted through the lunate. Appears radiographically as a cortical disruption through the body but is often poorly visualized in traditional x rays.

Adult, fall down with outstretched hand. A transverse fracture at the mid capitate in PA view.

Trapezoid and trapezium

Visible on PA view at the radial aspect of the distal carpal row. Somewhat overlapping each other so assessing outlines can be challenging.

Trapezium fracture

Uncommon but third most common of carpal bones to fracture. Generally requires high energy trauma. Typically results from axial loading causing vertical fracture line.

Adult, motor vehicle accident with direct trauma to the hand. Intraarticular trapezium fracture.

Trapezoid fracture

Rare injury. Typically requires high-energy trauma due to its protected position within the carpus.

Adult, wrist was hit by a ball. A longitudinal transparent fracture line visible through the trapezoid bone in oblique view.

Distal radioulnar joint (DRUJ)

DRUJ articulation

The normal druj appears as a narrow space between the curved sigmoid notch of the radius and the rounded ulnar head. The ulna is on average 0.5 mm shorter than the radius at the distal DRUJ level but variation occurs in either direction (ulnar variance). Assessment requires neutral wrist rotation (true PA), as pronation makes the radius appear shorter.

In a true PA view, the ulnar styloid appears in profile ulnarly with the extensor carpi ulnaris groove visible radially.

In the lateral view, the radius and ulna should overlap at DRUJ level - provided that the image is true lateral (the volar cortex of pisiform (red line) is seen between the anterior surfaces of the distal scaphoid and capitate (blue lines)).

Posterior DRUJ dislocation

Rare injury. Posterior is more common than anterior. Typically result from falling on hand or diect trauma. Can be associated with Galeazzi fracture-dislocations. On an AP view, the DRUJ space may appear widened. On lateral view, the ulnar head appears to be displaced dorsally relative to the radius, though technically it's the radius that is displaced volarly while the ulna maintains its position.

Adult, fall from bike. Dorsal distal radioulnar joint dislocation seen in lateral image.

Anterior DRUJ dislocation

Results from hypersupination injuries. On AP view, the DRUJ space may appear altered. On lateral view, the ulnar head appears to be displaced volarly (anteriorly) relative to the radius, though technically it's the radius that is displaced dorsally while the ulna maintains its position.

Adult, fall on outstreched hand. Volar dislocation of the distal radioulnar articulation.

DRUJ stabilizers

Soft tissue stabilizers of the DRUJ (e.g. TFCC, radioulnar ligaments) can't be visualized on X-ray and may be injured despite normal radiographic appearance of the joint. Indirect X-ray signs can include ulnar styloid fractures, positive ulnar variance, or subtle DRUJ widening. MRI or wrist arthroscopy are typically used for diagnosis.

Perilunar joints

The scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum form a functional unit in the proximal carpal row, connected by the scapholunate and lunotriquetral ligaments as primary stabilizers, with secondary stabilizers (e.g. dorsal radiocarpal ligaments, palmar ligaments, and midcarpal joint capsule) providing additional support. Injury to any component of this kinematic chain (bony/ligamentous) can disrupt the balanced forces across the wrist, creating malalignment patterns visible in x rays. While acute ligamentous injuries may not show immediate radiographic changes, they often evolve over time into characteristic malalignment patterns - affecting scapholunate, capitolunate and radiolunate angles. Perilunar injuries represent a continuum of injury to the ligaments/joints surrounding the lunate bone and can be ligamentous or combination of fracture and ligamentous injuries.

Scapholunate joint

The scapholunate interosseous ligament (SLIL) with the secondary stabilizers of the joint joins the scaphoid and lunate together. Normally the adult SL interval is less than 2-3 mm wide on PA view, though diagnostic thresholds can vary between sources. 

Normal lateral view: Scapholunate angle is between 30-60° = angle between the long axis of the scaphoid (white) and and a line perpendicular to the AP axis of the lunate (black).

Scapholunate dissociation

An acute traumatic SL dissociation may not be radiographically evident immediately after injury. The gap often becomes more evident as the secundary stabilizing structures weaken over time with continued wrist use. Typical injury mechanism is fall on outstretched hand in sports injuries and falls.

When evolved, the PA view shows widened SL gap >3mm (Terry-Thomas sign), ring sign of rotated scaphoid. Lateral view shows increased scapholunate angle >60-70°

Adult, Fell from a ladder with outstretched hand. Widening of distance between scaphoid and lunate to greater than 3 mm "Terry-Thomas sign". There's also the "ring sign" on the scaphoid. Scapholunate angle is >70°.

Capitolunate articulation

The normal capitolunate articulation consists of the convex proximal pole of the capitate nesting within the concave distal surface of the lunate. In PA image it appears as a uniform joint space forming part of the continuous midcarpal arc.
In the lateral view the capitate, lunate, and the radius should line up colinearly, indicating an intact lunocapitate articulation. Normal capitolunate angle is 0±15° = angle between the longitudinal axis of capitate (white) and a line perpendicular to the AP axis of the lunate (black).

Perilunate dislocation

The capitate-lunate articulation is disrupted. Typically high-energy trauma; falls from height or motor vehicle accidents. In the AP view the capitate and lunate seem to overlap and the joint space is not seen. (disrupted carpal arcs). In lateral view capitate is not in line with lunate but dorsal to it, while lunate maintains articulation with radius.

Trauma. The capitate-lunate articulation is disrupted while the radial-capitate articulation is maintained. There is dorsal displacement of the distal carpal row.

Lunotriquetral joint

The lunotriquetral ligament complex, including the lunotriquetral interosseous ligament (LTIL), joins the lunate and triquetral bones. PA view shows continuous lunotriquetral joint space without widening or step-off, with maintained carpal alignment.

Lateral view: Triquetrum overlaps with other carpal bones and it's alignment is not easily assessed. Normal scapholunate angle (30-60), normal capitolunate angle and normal radiolunate angle speak against significant LT injury.

Lunotriquetral disruption

Fall on outstretched hand with wrist in extension, radial deviation, and intercarpal pronation; can occur with ulnar styloid fractures; typically moderate energy trauma. PA view: Widened LT interval, triquetrum may appear proximally translated. Lateral view: VISI pattern with lunate volarly tilted, decreased scapholunate angle.

Example of perilunate fracture-dislocation, where the capitate has dislocated dorsally from the lunate (lateral image). The lunate, although rotated anteriorly, still connects with the radius. Fractures of triquetrum and ulnar styloid process are seen.

Radiolunate articulation

Normal radiographic appearance of radiolunate articulation in PA view: Lunate appears quadrilateral and is proximally partly superimposed with the radial articular surface (lunate fossa).

In lateral view the lunate is aligned with distal radius articular surface and capitate. Normal radiolunate angle is 0±15° = angle between the longitudinal axis of radius (white) and a line perpendicular to the AP axis of the lunate (black).

Lunate dislocation

High-energy trauma pattern ( falls from significant height, crush injuries, or high-velocity impacts); represents final stage of perilunate instability. A severe ligamentous injury, where lunate dislocates typically volarly as the dorsal radiocarpal ligaments fail. Radiologically this is seen as triangular lunate overlapping capitate in PA image. In lateral image lunate is displaced and angulated volarly. If the lunate dislocates into the carpal tunnel an acute compression of the median nerve follows.

PA: Lunate appears triangular (“piece of pie” sign). In lateral image lunate is seen dislocated anteriorly. Radius and capitate are in line. Dorsal radiolunate ligament injured.

Associated injuries

Median nerve injury

Median nerve damage due to blunt wrist trauma usually results from strain or compression of the nerve within the carpal tunnel, often due to a hematoma or fracture fragments. Typical causes are dislocated distal radius fractures, perilunate/lunate dislocations and sometimes (rarely) carpal bone fractures (scaphoid, capitate).

Carpal bones relation to the median nerve and the carpal tunnel.

Ulnar nerve injury

Ulnar nerve injury related to blunt wrist trauma is rarer than the median nerve involvement. The ulnar nerve runs through the Guyons canal, where it can be compressed due to hamate fracture for example.

Carpal bones relation to the ulnar nerve and the Guyons canal.

Vascular injuries

Vascular injuries caused by blunt trauma of the wrist are rare. The vasculature of the wrist and hand is supplied by radial and ulnar arteries and their connecting arches.

AVN of scaphoid

Scaphoid fractures, especially the proximal pole, can lead to avascular necrosis due to retrograde blood supply of the bone. Thorough initial x-ray examination, including scaphoid views, is important. Yet, if a fracture is not visible but the clinical suspicion is high, further imaging (CT) may be needed, or the wrist may be immobilized for a later x-ray.

title: Wrist joint injection (radiocarpal joint)

source_knowledge:
VideoDoctor: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist)
StepsPreparations and patient positioningThe patient can sit with the affected hand face-down on their lap, supported by a cushion. Alternatively, they can lie back with the hand in the same position.

For example, an orange needle 25 G (0.5 x 16 mm) or a blue needle 23 G (0.6 x 30) mm can be used. 

For anti-inflammatory treatment and for alleviating symptoms of osteoarthritis 0.5-1 ml methylprednisolone 40 mg/ml can be used. 

Landmarks and injection siteThe aim is to find a safe injection site into the radiocarpal joint. This ensures that arthrocentesis or treatments are done accurately into the joint, while also lessening the chance of injuring nearby tendons or neurovascular structures.
Palpate the distal end of the radius on the dorsal side.

By having the patient lift their index finger, the extensor digitorum tendon is traced to the radius's distal end. 

The injection site is identified slightly radially to the tendon at the radius's distal end.

There are no significant neurovascular structures in this area, which makes it an ideal spot for injecting.


Mark the entry site.

InjectionClean the skin area and let it dry.

Insert the needle perpendicularly to the skin or slightly ulnarly, underneath the extensor digitorum tendon. The depth of insertion is about 1.5 – 2 cm from the skin surface.

Inject the medicinal agent appropriate to the situation. There is typically very low resistance in the joint space.

Synovial fluid analysis (if a sample is taken) The turbidity and color of the synovial fluid can be assessed visually. Fairly clear synovial fluid with visible measurement markings on the syringe is a sign of no pronounced infection. Cloudy synovial fluid is caused by a large number of white blood cells making the measurement markings invisible.

If the cause of joint effusion is unclear, take a sample if possible.Cell count + crystal analysis and bacterial culture If antibiotic treatment has already initiated and septic arthritis is strongly suspected, request a bacterial nucleic acid analysis. If Lyme disease -related joint condition is suspected, request a Borrelia nucleic acid analysis. If only a drop of synovial fluid is available, it's possible to take a bacterial swab sample. 

AftercareGeneral guidelines:
Cover the injection site with an adhesive bandage and keep it dry and clean for 24 hours to minimize the risk of infection. 
For a better therapeutic effect, avoid stress of the injected joint for 24-48 hours and avoid heavy strain for one week. 
Glucocorticoids are absorbed more into the bloodstream, particularly from large weight-bearing joints, if the joint is stressed.

General

Medical expert: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist)

Name of Procedure: Wrist joint injection (radiocarpal joint), wrist joint puncture, wrist arthrocentensis, wrist corticosteroid injection



Goal

Safe access to the wrist joint (radiocarpal joint) for synovial fluid sampling and local treatment. 

Problem

Unspecific wrist joint effusion or the need for local treatment of an inflammatory joint condition, gout or osteoarthritis.

Diagnosis

M13.9 Arthritis, unspecified 

M05.8 Other rheumatoid arthritis with rheumatoid factor 

M19.8 Other specified arthrosis 

Short pathophysiological description

The wrist joints consist of the radiocarpal, midcarpal and distal radioulnar joints. The radiocarpal joint, which is the primary focus here, is the most frequently inflamed and symptomatic of the wrist joints. 

The radiocarpal joint is commonly inflamed in conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, gout and psoriatic arthritis. Inflammatory conditions can lead to synovial hypertrophy and an increased production of synovial fluid in the joint. 

Significant osteoarthritis of the wrist joint may develop, particularly after a trauma. 

Key anatomical structures 

Radiocarpal joint

Radius 

Extensor digitorum tendon 

Pitfalls 

Wrong injection site 

If the injection is given too far on the radial side, there is a risk of hitting the radial artery and the radial nerve in the anatomical snuffbox. 

The extensor tendons of the wrist and the digits should be avoided as injecting through these is extremely painful. 

The superficial veins should also be avoided when giving the injection in order to avoid local hematoma. 





Challenges with sample aspiration 

If the joint capsule is thickened, synovial debris may block the needle when aspirating. The risk increases with the aspiration force and syringe volume. Try changing to a smaller syringe. You can also try to unblock the needle tip by injecting a small amount of fluid into the joint and then reaspirating. 

If sample aspiration is unsuccessful when acute gout is suspected, try saline irrigation. After injecting 1 ml of fluid try aspirating again. This will also yield a sample for bacterial analysis. 



Storage of a synovial fluid sample 

A synovial fluid sample should be analyzed promptly after taking the sample. Synovial fluid containing blood will rapidly coagulate. There may be cell degradation in the sample after just an hour. Crystals, on the other hand, are inorganic salts and are better preserved and so their analysis can be made the next day. 



Injecting too frequently 

In case of acute inflammation involving a small joint, like the radiocarpal joint, intra-articular injections can be given at 1-month intervals for the first 3 months. Afterwards, if needed, 6 weeks apart. 

There is no strong evidence of adverse effects of glucocorticoids on the joint, although frequent injections may cause unwanted systemic effects.

Complications

Infection 

Intra-articular infection after a joint injection is very rare, the incidence being 1:2000-1:100 000, most commonly 1:10 000. Nevertheless, maintain aseptic technique while doing the procedure. 



Joint pain 

Joint pain for 24 hours post-injection is an adverse effect occurring in up to 10% of cases, particularly with glucocorticoid injections. It may result from a local tissue injury caused by the needle alone but is usually due to an irritation reaction to the crystalline glucocorticoid or hyaluronate. The joint may be painful, perhaps even feel warm, for about 24 hours, but these symptoms ease spontaneously. The symptoms can be alleviated by analgesics or cold treatment. 



Hemarthrosis 

Intra-articular hemorrhage is a very rare complication of a joint injection. Antithrombotic treatment or anticoagulant medication is not a contraindication for intra-articular injection as the risk is very small, about 0-2%, even in patients using these medicines. In patients on anticoagulants, however, the smallest possible needle is recommended for joint injection. 



Local skin atrophy or hypopigmentation 

Local skin atrophy occurs in about 1% of the patients after intra-articular injection of glucocorticoids. Typically, atrophy at the injection area develops at 1-4 months post-injection. In addition to atrophy, there may be hypopigmentation around the injection area. Atrophy and hypopigmentation are caused by subcutaneous glucocorticoid accumulation, potentially due to extracapsular injection or extracapsular seepage of the glucocorticoid via the puncture canal. 



Tendon tear 

Tendon tears during intra-articular injections are very rare and are often due to unintentional injection of glucocorticoid into the adjacent tendon rather than the joint itself. To avoid tendon injuries, glucocorticoids should always be injected against low resistance. 



Pericapsular calcium deposit 

Pericapsular calcification, or calcium deposits around the joint capsule of the target joint is very common, occurring after intra-articular injections in up to 10% of cases. Pericapsular calcium deposits are usually asymptomatic and harmless. In most cases, calcium is spontaneously absorbed from the synovial capsule but in some patients X-ray images may show even permanent calcifications, particularly in small joints such as the PIP and the DIP joints of the fingers. 



Compromise of diabetes management 

Following intra-articular injections, some of the glucocorticoid dose will be absorbed into the systemic bloodstream and in diabetic patients, for example, blood sugar level might be elevated for a few days post-injection. The risk is highest for short-acting agents or when multiple injections are given together. Patients with diabetes should be advised to step up their blood sugar monitoring post-injection, and if necessary to adjust their diabetes medication doses temporarily.

title: Vascular injury management

Clamps are placed proximal and distal to the injury.  The vessel is cut.  The suture thread is looped around the vessel just proximal to the clamp.  The first knot is tied and the clamp is carefully opened and removed while simultaneously securing the knot.  Additional knots (4-6) are tied.  The ends of the thread...
source_knowledge:
VideoSurgeon: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon)
Steps

Immediate response to a bleeding vesselPressure applied with a finger

For minor surface bleed: electrocautery

For larger raw surface bleeding: local hemostatic agents

Simple ligation of a vessel

The bleeding vessel is exposed.

Clamps are placed proximal and distal to the injury.

The vessel is cut.

The suture thread is looped around the vessel just proximal to the clamp.

The first knot is tied and the clamp is carefully opened and removed while simultaneously securing the knot.

Additional knots (4-6) are tied.

The ends of the thread are cut

The other end of the vessel is treated in a similar manner.

Transfixing ligature

The artery is exposed, clamped, and cut.

A needle is passed through the vessel, piercing both walls, just proximal to the clamp.

The thread is pulled through and looped around half of the vessel.

The first knot is tied, sealing half of the vessel.

The thread is looped around the remaining part of the vessel.

The knot is tied and the clamp is released while the knot is simultaneously while secured.

Additional knots (4-6) are tied.

The threads are cut.

Arteriar repair principlesProximal and distal control of the artery is obtained, and the injury is evaluated as suitable for direct repair.

The transverse closure is started from inside out, including the intima layer on the takes.

A thread with a needle attached to both ends is used so that suturing can continue uninterrupted on the other side.

The ends are connected and tied.

Multiple knots (6-7) are tied.

Suturing (running or interrupted) is continued, taking small bites including the intima layer each time.

Before the thread is secured, some blood and possible air are expelled by carefully releasing the clamp.

Multiple knots are tied and the ends of the thread are cut.

title: Urethral catheter placement
secondary_keywords: foley catheter insertion technique; male urethral catheterization steps; difficult catheter placement technique; sterile urinary catheter insertion; catheter placement clinical procedure
all_keywords: urethral catheter placement procedure; foley catheter insertion technique; male urethral catheterization steps; difficult catheter placement technique; sterile urinary catheter insertion; catheter placement clinical procedure

source_knowledge:
Patient positioning, anesthesia and preparationInserting the catheterInflating the balloonSurgeon: Petrus Järvinen (urologist)
Steps Patient positioning and preparation The patient is supine with legs slightly apart.

The foreskin, if present, is retracted and the glans, the sulcus behind it, and the urethral meatus are swabbed.

The urethra is lubricated with a local anesthetic gel, such as lidocaine gel, which also aids in dilating the urethral passage and providing some degree of anesthesia. The lubricant is applied generously (e.g., 2 tubes) directly into the urethra.

Inserting the catheterThe standard urinary catheter is a 2-way silicone Foley in size 14-16 Fr for adult males. Upsizing is recommended in conditions like hematuria with clots. A 3-way catheter is selected when irrigation is needed. A Coude (curved) tip is considered if the insertion is difficult.

To straighten the urethra's natural curve before catheter insertion, the penis should be oriented upwards.

The catheter is inserted into the urethra with care, ensuring it slides forward smoothly.

Some resistance can be felt around the prostate area. The urethra also naturally curves in a more ventral direction before reaching the prostate level.

If insertion becomes difficult at this point, gentle pressure applied to the perineum can assist in advancing the catheter.

When the catheter has reached the bladder, the outflow of urine will confirm correct placement.

The catheter should be inserted fully to ensure that the balloon is positioned within the bladder and not at the level of the urethra.

Inflating the balloon
In a dual-lumen catheter, one channel facilitates the outflow of urine, while the other is used to inflate the balloon.

The balloon is inflated with sterile water, typically 10 ml, or as indicated on the catheter itself.Exceeding this volume should be avoided to prevent the balloon from bursting.

Resistance felt during inflation may indicate incorrect balloon placement, potentially causing urethral damage.

Once the balloon is inflated, the catheter can be gently pulled back to ensure the balloon is fully inflated and correctly positioned within the bladder.

The foreskin, if present, should be returned to its original position to prevent the development of paraphimosis.

AftercareGeneral guidelines: 

A closed sterile drainage system is maintained to prevent contamination and infection.

The catheter tube is secured to prevent movement and urethral traction. The catheter and collecting tube are kept free from kinking.

The duration a urethral catheter is left in place can vary based on individual circumstances and medical needs. However, it's important to keep the catheterization period as short as possible to minimize risks of urinary tract infections and damage to the urethra (e.g. erosion).

General

Medical expert: Petrus Järvinen (urologist)

Names of Procedure: urethral catheterization, urinary catheter placement, Foley catheter placement, bladder catheterization

Goal of operation

To facilitate bladder drainage or access by inserting a catheter through the urethra into the bladder.

Problem

Typically: retention, hematuria or preoperative preparation

Diagnosis

Urinary retention (ICD-10: R33)

Hematuria (ICD-10: R31)



Short pathophysiological description

Urethral catheterization provides a direct pathway for urine to exit the body when normal urination is not possible. By bypassing obstructions or compensating for lack of bladder control, it allows for the immediate relief of urinary retention. This not only alleviates discomfort but also prevents potential kidney damage from backflow of urine. In cases of hematuria, catheterization flushes out blood clots that may be blocking the urinary tract.

Key anatomical structures

Urethra

Bladder

Prostate

Pitfalls

Insufficient lubrication

Insufficient lubrication can make catheter insertion difficult, potentially leading to an unsuccessful procedure. Additionally, it can increase patient discomfort and risk of urethral trauma.

Forceful insertion

The catheter should never be inserted forcefully, as this can result in creation of a false passage or urethral perforation. If resistance is encountered and cannot be resolved with gentle manipulation or additional lubrication, an evaluation should be done to identify the cause, which could include a urethral stricture, prostate obstruction, bladder stones, a foreign body, or a tumor.

Premature balloon inflation

If the balloon is inflated prematurely, while still within the urethra, typically in the prostate, it can cause significant trauma and pain. To avoid this, the catheter should be fully inserted first, then inflated, and only then pulled back to check the hold.

Long term use

Prolonged catheterization increases the risk of urinary tract infections, urethral damage, erosion and bladder stones. Catheters should be used for the shortest time possible and regular assessments should be conducted to determine if continued use is necessary.

Complications

Urethtral trauma

Potential damage to the urethral tissue may occur during catheter placement leading to urethral strictures, hematuria, false passages, and increased risk of infections. This can be mitigated by ensuring the catheter is inserted and removed gently, and by using sufficient lubrication. If clear resistance is met, the procedure should be stopped and reassessed.

False passage

False passage refers to an artificial channel creation into the periurethral tissues. The risk increases with prior urethral alterations due to instrumentation, trauma, radiation therapy or infections. To prevent this, a correct catheterization technique, involving appropriate catheter choice, sufficient lubrication, and gentle insertion should be applied.

Urethral erosion

Urethral erosion may occur due to prolonged catheter use. This risk can be minimized by ensuring the catheter is not left in place longer than necessary and by regular monitoring for signs of urethral injury.

Urinary track infections

Urinary catheterization can lead to urinary tract infections, especially when the catheter remains in place for a long time. The insertion can introduce bacteria into the urinary tract, and damage to the urethra can increase UTI risk. To minimize the risk, a proper insertion technique, aseptic protocol, and the removal of the catheter as soon as possible are essential.

title: Trigger Finger Release
secondary_keywords: open trigger finger release steps; a1 pulley release procedure; trigger finger surgery approach; stenosing tenosynovitis release technique; trigger finger operative steps

source_knowledge:
Skin IncisionVisualize the tendon sheathVisualize and split the A1 pulleyVerify the goal and check for damageClosureSurgeon: Matti Mustaniemi (hand surgeon)

StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparation The patient is in supine position with the affected limb positioned laterally, hand flat (in supination) on the operation table. 
Local anesthesia. Inject local anesthetic (e.g. lidocain) slowly with a 10ml syringe at the distal palmar crease over the A1 pulley. 
A tourniquet with a pressure setting of 100mmHg above systolic pressure can be used for hemorrhage control. Alternatively use a local anesthetic supplemented with adrenaline. 

Landmarks Inspect and palpate the landmarks: the flexor tendon and its sheath, as well as the distal palmar crease. The A1 pulley is located at the level of MCP joint, which can be easily felt from the dorsal side of the hand. Fingers should be held extended and the hand flat on the operation table to move the neurovascular structures dorsally. 

Skin incision Make a zig-zag (Bruner) skin incision starting above the level of the distal palmar crease, advancing to the crease and turning on it. Each leg of the incision should be about 10-15mm in length. 

Visualize the tendon sheath Coagulate any bleeding vessels in and immediately below the skin (bipolar cautery forceps). 
Use finger retractors to retract the skin and the subcutaneous fat radially and ulnarly. 

Use tenotomy scissors to advance through the subcutaneous fat tissue. Using the retractors, advance until you reach the tendon sheath while holding the fat aside, in order to maintain good visibility.

Visualize and split the A1 pulley Use a scalpel to split the A1 pulley. Its distal and proximal edges are easily inspected once opened. 

Verify the goal has been accomplished and ensure hemostasis Verify that no stiffness remains and the tendon is free. The patient should now be asked to flex the finger in order to make sure no triggering occurs. 

Release tourniquet, if used, and coagulate any bleeding vessels (bipolar cautery forceps).

Wound closure The only layer that requires suturing is the skin. Use 4-0 or 5-0 interrupted monofilament sutures. 

Apply a non-bulky hand surgical dressing that permits maximal postoperative hand mobility. 

Aftercare General guidelines: For most patients early postoperative mobilization and resuming daily activities are recommendable. Local soreness, swelling and scarring of the area are common postoperative symptoms, however, these usually recede within the following months.

General

Medical expert: Matti Mustaniemi (hand surgeon)

Name of procedure: Trigger finger release 

Goal of operation

To relieve pressure on the finger flexor tendons at the A1 pulley by splitting the pulley. 

Problem

Inflammation and stiffness at the A1 pulley of the finger flexor tendon sheath. 

Diagnosis

Trigger finger (ICD-code: M65.3) 



Short pathophysiological description

Loading, physical stress, trauma and/or disease may cause hypertrophy, edema and fibrosis to the flexor tendon and its surrounding soft tissues. This can restrict the normal gliding of the tendon in the tendon sheath. Symptoms begin as slight pain and may progress via a clicking sensation (or “triggering”), when moving the finger, to complete locking (inability to extend). Symptoms vary from very mild to completely impairing. The condition is most common in digits IV and III. Failure of conservative treatment indicates operative treatment. 

Key anatomical structures 

Distal palmar crease 

Flexor tendons 

Palmar aponeurosis 

Tendon sheath and its pulleys 

Intermetacarpal neurovascular bundles 

Pitfalls 

Incorrect site of incision 

The incision is commonly made too distally, which can lead to opening or partially damaging the wrong (A2) pulley. This can be avoided by locating the A1 pulley and marking it before performing the incision.



Crossing the crease perpendicularly could cause contracting scarring that can severely impair hand function, so, the incision should zigzag, using a Bruner incision. 

Improper hand positioning

Laying the hand flat brings the flexor tendon closer to the skin in relation to the neurovascular bundles. If the fingers are flexed during surgery, there is a higher risk of neurovascular damage. To avoid damage to the neurovascular bundle please ask your assistant to hold the finger extended during the procedure. 

Carless tissue handling

Splitting the A1 pulley should be done with caution to prevent damage to the underlying flexor tendon.

Complications

Neurovascular damage 

Failure to hold the hand flat (fingers extended) while visualizing the tendon sheath and the A1 pulley may lead to neurovascular damage. Keeping the fingers extended moves the neurovascular bundles towards the dorsal side of the hand. 

Damage to the radial digital nerve of the thumb 

If operating on the thumb, be aware that the radial digital nerve of the thumb can cross the operative field and should be protected when visualized. 

Tendon laceration 

Incising the pulley too deep when visualizing and splitting the A1 pulley may cause tendon laceration. 

Incomplete A1 pulley release 

Incomplete A1 pulley release is possible if the edges of the pulley are inadequately exposed. 

Damaging or releasing the A2 pulley 

Excessive incising may damage the A2 pulley, which could lead to tendon bowstringing and pain. 



In some cases, the A2 pulley might be opened instead of the A1 pulley due to disorientation. This can be prevented by carefully making the skin incision according to the right landmarks.

title: Traumatic finger amputation revision
secondary_keywords: traumatic finger amputation management; revision amputation finger procedure; fingertip amputation revision steps; digital amputation surgical technique; traumatic finger revision surgery
all_keywords: finger amputation revision technique; traumatic finger amputation management; revision amputation finger procedure; fingertip amputation revision steps; digital amputation surgical technique; traumatic finger revision surgery
