source_knowledge:
Video
Doctor: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist)

Steps Preparations and patient positioning The patient is seated with the arm relaxed at the side. 

For example, a blue needle 23 G (0.6 x 30 mm) or a green needle 21 G (0.8 x 40 mm) can be used. 

Landmarks and injection site 
The posterior injection site for the glenohumeral joint is located by first palpating the spine of the scapula, which is usually quite prominent. This is followed laterally to the acromion.

The acromial angle, a prominent bony point at the junction of the lateral border of the acromion and the spine of the scapula, is easily palpated as it feels quite sharp and distinct.

This point can be marked for reference.

Using the acromial angle as a landmark, the injection site is located by moving about 2 centimeters downward and about one centimeter medially. 

To inject into the glenohumeral joint space, the needle is directed medially using the palpable coracoid process anteriorly as a landmark. Inserting perpendicular to the skin would hit the humeral head due to the angled orientation of the joint space.

Superior view 

The injection site can be marked with a blunt pen, for example.

Injection The injection site is cleaned and allowed to dry before the injection is performed.

The coracoid process is palpated from the anterior side to establish the proper trajectory when inserting the needle. 

The needle is inserted about 2 to 3 centimeters deep to reach the joint space.

Aspiration is performed to examine for synovial fluid and confirm proper needle placement. In osteoarthritis, synovial fluid may be minimal or absent.

The appropriate medicinal agent is injected according to the clinical situation. Resistance is typically minimal in the joint space. 

Synovial fluid analysis (if a sample is taken) The turbidity and color of the synovial fluid can be assessed visually. Fairly clear synovial fluid with visible scale is a sign of no pronounced infection. Cloudy synovial fluid is caused by a large number of white blood cells making the scale marks invisible. 

If the cause joint effusion is unclear, synovial fluid should be obtained for diagnostic analysis.
Synovial fluid analysis: cell count with differential, crystal analysis, and bacterial cultureIf synovial fluid volume is limited, a bacterial swab may be obtained as an alternative for culture.In cases of suspected septic arthritis with prior antibiotic exposure, bacterial PCR may be needed as cultures can be falsely negative after antibiotic treatment.When Lyme arthritis is suspected, Borrelia PCR can help confirm the diagnosis.

AftercareGeneral guidelines The injection site is covered with an adhesive bandage and kept dry and clean for 24 hours to minimize infection risk.

For optimal therapeutic effect, stress on the injected joint is avoided for 24-48 hours, with heavy strain avoided for one week.

General 

Medical expert: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist)

Name of procedure: Shoulder joint injection, shoulder joint puncture, glenohumeral joint injection, glenohumeral injection, shoulder injection

Goal of operation 

Safe access to glenohumeral joint for synovial fluid sampling, if needed, and local treatment.

Problem 

Unspecific shoulder joint effusion or the need for local treatment of an inflammatory joint condition, gout or osteoarthritis.

Diagnosis 

M13.9 Arthritis, unspecified 

M05.8 Other rheumatoid arthritis with rheumatoid factor 

M19.8 Other specified arthrosis 

Short pathophysiological description 

The glenohumeral joint, also known as the shoulder joint, is commonly inflamed in conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, and reactive arthritis. Inflammatory conditions can lead to synovial hypertrophy and an increased production of synovial fluid in the joint. 

Glenohumeral injections are a common therapeutic approach for these conditions. They involve the direct injection of medicinal agents (usually corticosteroids and/or a local anesthetic) into the joint space. This helps to reduce inflammation, alleviate pain, and improve joint function. 

Key anatomical structures 

Glenohumeral joint

Scapula 

Glenoid

Acromial angle 

Spine of scapula

Coracoid process

Humeral head

Pitfalls 

Wrong injection trajectory

If the injection is not angled towards the coracoid process, the tip of the needle can hit the humeral head and not enter the joint space. Before injecting, the coracoid process on the anterior side is palpated, and the needle aimed towards it. 

Challenges with sample aspiration 

If sample aspiration is unsuccessful when acute gout is suspected, saline irrigation may be attempted. After 1 ml of fluid is injected, aspiration is repeated. This approach also provides a sample for bacterial analysis.

Storage of the synovial fluid sample 

A synovial fluid sample should be analysed promptly after taking the sample. Synovial fluid containing blood will rapidly coagulate. There may be cell degradation in the sample after just an hour. Crystals, on the other hand, are inorganic salts and are better preserved and so their analysis can be made the next day. 

Injecting too frequently 

In case of acute inflammation, intra-articular injections can be given at 1-month intervals for the first 3 months. Afterwards, if needed, 3 months apart. 

There is no strong evidence of adverse effects of glucocorticoids on the joint, although frequent injections may cause unwanted systemic effects.

Complications 

Infection 

Intra-articular infection after a joint injection is very rare, the incidence being 1:2000-1:100 000, most commonly 1:10 000. Nevertheless, maintain aseptic technique while doing the procedure. 

Joint pain 

Joint pain during 24 hours post-injection is an adverse effect occurring in up to 10% of cases, particularly with glucocorticoid injections. It may result from a local tissue injury caused by the needle alone but is usually due to an irritation reaction to the crystalline glucocorticoid or hyaluronate. The joint may be painful, perhaps even feel warm, for about 24 hours, but these symptoms ease spontaneously. The symptoms can be alleviated by analgesics or cold treatment.

Hemarthrosis 

Intra-articular hemorrhage is a very rare complication of a joint injection. Antithrombotic treatment or anticoagulant medication is not a contraindication for intra-articular injection as the risk is very small, about 0-2%, even in patients using these medicines. In patients on anticoagulants, however, the smallest possible needle is recommended for joint injection. 

Local skin atrophy or hypopigmentation 

Local skin atrophy occurs in about 1% of the patients after intra-articular injection of glucocorticoids. Typically, atrophy at the injection area develops at 1-4 months post-injection. In addition to atrophy, there may be hypopigmentation around the injection area. Atrophy and hypopigmentation are caused by subcutaneous glucocorticoid accumulation, potentially due to extracapsular injection or extracapsular seepage of the glucocorticoid via the puncture canal. 

Tendon tear 

Tendon tears during intra-articular injections are very rare and are often due to unintentional injection of glucocorticoid into the adjacent tendon rather than the joint itself. To avoid tendon injuries, glucocorticoids should always be injected against low resistance. 

Pericapsular calcium deposit 

Pericapsular calcification, or calcium deposits around the joint capsule of the target joint is a very common, occurring after intra-articular injections in up to 10% of cases. Pericapsular calcium deposits are usually asymptomatic and harmless. In most cases, calcium is spontaneously absorbed from the synovial capsule but in some patients X-ray images may show even permanent calcifications, particularly in small joints such as the PIP and the DIP joints of the fingers. 

Compromise of diabetes management 

Following intra-articular injections, some of the glucocorticoid dose will be absorbed into the systemic bloodstream and in diabetic patients, for example, blood sugar level might be elevated for a few days post-injection. The risk is highest for short-acting agents or when multiple injections are given together. Patients with diabetes should be advised to step up their blood sugar monitoring post-injection, and if necessary to adjust their diabetes medication doses temporarily.

title: Scrotal exploration and orchiopexy

source_knowledge:
Patient positioning, preparations and anesthesiaApproach goal and anatomical startegyLandmarks and incision siteSkin incision and advancing to testicular surfaceTesticle delivery, detorsion & vitality assessmentOrchiopexyHemostasis and closureSurgeon: Petrus Järvinen (urologist)

Scrotal exploration is performed because of suspected or proven spermatic cord torsion and resultant testicular ischemia. If untreated, testicular torsion can lead to testicular necrosis requiring orchiectomy due to irreversible ischemic damage.
The goal of the procedure is to achieve detorsion of the spermatic cord to restore testicular perfusion, assess tissue viability, and perform bilateral orchiopexy to prevent recurrent torsion.
StepsPatient positioning, preparation and anesthesiaThe patient is supine with legs slightly abducted.
The draping should allow access to both sides of the scrotum for bilateral orchiopexy. The penis is positioned away from operative area. It can be secured on the abdomen with surgical drape or tape in a 12 o’clock position. 

General anesthesia is the standard form of anesthesia. 

Approach goal and strategyThe goal of the approach is to access the testicle and spermatic cord within the scrotum.

Using the midline approach, this is accomplished by making an incision through the scrotal skin and dividing the multiple layers of the scrotal wall, opening the tunica vaginalis and identifying the tunica albuginea, which confirms that the testicular surface has been reached, followed by delivery of the testicle.

Critical structures that must be preserved include the vas deferens within the spermatic cord and the testicular artery and venous plexus providing vascular supply.

Landmarks and incision site

The affected testicle is palpated to confirm its position, size and possible edema. Palpation of the spermatic cord may reveal evidence of twisting, particularly in lean patients.

The raphe of scrotum is identified on the skin, marking the midline where the internal scrotal septum divides the scrotum into bilateral compartments.

The incision is placed anteriorly along the midline using the raphe as a landmark.The incision length should be sufficient to deliver the testicle and perform orchiopexy, typically 3-4 cm in pediatric patients or 4-5 cm in adults, corresponding roughly to testicular dimensions.

Skin incision and advancing to testicular surfaceThe goal is to advance through the multiple layers of the scrotal wall to the testicular surface covered by the tunica albuginea.

The skin is placed under tension with the assisting hand to facilitate the incision.

Either scalpel or diathermy can be used for the incision which is made along the planned line through the skin layer.

Hemostasis should be secured as needed during the advancement to maintain visibility.

Following the skin incision, the underlying scrotal wall layers appear as multiple thin membranes.

During dissection through the layers, Crile forceps can be employed to elevate the layersin a tent-like manner, preventing inadvertent damage to the underlying testicular surface.

The testicle is elevated and positioned against the wound to enable better visualization and grasping of the subsequent layer. Electrocautery could also be employed to divide the tissue planes.

As dissection progresses, the remaining layers become translucent and the tunica albuginea becomes visible underneath. If uncertain, forceps can test whether graspable tissue remains, indicating further dissection is needed.

When reaching the testicle surface, the tunica albuginea appears distinctly different from the superficial layers, appearing as a fibrous capsule that directly surrounds the testicular parenchyma.

The tunica vaginalis (parietal layer), which is the final layer to divide, is incised along the entire length of the wound.

Small amount of peritoneal-type fluid normally exists in this space serving as an additional landmark indicating that the correct anatomical plane has been reached. 

Testicle delivery, detorsion and vitality assessmentThe goal is to confirm whether testicular torsion is present and determine if the testicle is viable.

The testicle is delivered and explored to assess.

Torsion may be apparent on visual inspection when the testicle appears darkened or discolored rather than normal pale pink, indicating ischemia. Extra turns in the spermatic cord may also be visible and palpable.

If torsion is evident, the first step is to perform detorsion as quickly as possible by removing the twists. This is done in the direction that allows the easiest unwinding, as rotating in the wrong direction would increase the number of turns. Testicular torsion typically involves 180° to over 720° of rotation and can occur in either medial or lateral direction.

Following successful detorsion, the spermatic cord becomes thinner and the twists disappear. At best the color returns immediately when the testicle is still viable as the straightened spermatic cord enables reperfusion and oxygen supply.

Returning of the color is a positive sign for viability and indication to proceed to orchiopexy.

If the color does not return immediately, the next step is to wait several minutes and apply warm gauzes to stimulate circulation.

If color fails to return despite warming and waiting several minutes, a small test incision on the testicle surface may be performed to assess any bleeding that would indicate viable circulation - bright red blood indicates viable arterial circulation while dark clotted blood suggests ischemic tissue. The test incision, if performed, should be closed with monofilament suture.

If the testicle shows clear necrosis with absolutely no viability, orchiectomy should be considered to prevent prolonged post-operative pain and tenderness and possible infection complications from necrotic tissue. 

In uncertain cases of viability, orchiectomy should not be done.

If the testicle requires removal, the spermatic cord should be ligated with multiple takes and the ligatures must be securely tied to prevent bleeding complications.

If no torsion is identified during the initial examination, alternative diagnoses are considered. Epididymitis or torsion of the appendix of testis or epididymis can mimick testicular torsion.
Epididymitis presents with a tender and swollen epididymis. 

Testicular appendages including the appendix testis and appendix epididymis are embryological remnants and variably present. They may also undergo torsion, especially when prominent, causing the appendage to twist around its pedicle and produce significant pain.

A testicular mass may indicate tumor, which can occasionally present with acute pain.

Assessment should also include evaluation for bell clapper deformity, where the testicle lacks normal posterior fixation to the tunica vaginalis, allowing easier rotation within the scrotal compartment and predisposing to torsion. This finding typically warrants bilateral orchiopexy even if no current torsion is present.

OrchiopexyThe objective is to secure the testicle to the scrotal wall to prevent future torsion. When torsion is confirmed, bilateral orchiopexy is typically performed given the often bilateral nature of the anatomical predisposition. If no torsion is identified it's typical to fixate the side explored.

For bilateral orchiopexy, contralateral access is achieved through the same skin incision by pressing the opposite testicle against the wound to tension the layers, divide them similarly and allow delivery.

Prior to starting orchiopexy, it should be confirmed that detorsion is complete to prevent the testicle being fixed in a twisted position.

Various fixation techniques exist for orchiopexy, but here the sutures are placed from the midline septum to the testicle surface. 

The fixation should be done using non-absorbable sutures. Multiple sutures -preferably three to four- prevent rotation around a single point and provide backup if sutures fail.

Superficial bites are taken from the median septum to avoid injury to contralateral vascular structures.

The testicular side sutures are placed through the tunica albuginea layer.Here also excessively deep bites are avoided not to compromise testicular circulation.

The suturing is easiest to perform by first placing all the bites and then tightening them afterwards.

The sutures are secured with multiple knots.

After the fixation sutures are secured, the testicle is returned to its anatomical position.

Hemostasis and closureMeticulous hemostasis is essential before closing the wound. Bleeding is one the most common complications after scrotal surgery and may lead to reoperations and infections.

The scrotal cavity can hold considerable blood volume and even small bleeds can lead to problematic hematomas requiring surgical evacuation.

The internal layer is closed with absorbable sutures using a continuous technique.

The skin layer is closed with rapidly absorbable sutures, for instance using interrupted suturing technique.

AftercareGeneral guidelines: Bruising, swelling, and tenderness may occur in the groin area for several days. Some patients may benefit from using scrotal support for the week following surgery. Vigorous activity should be avoided for two weeks after surgery. Sexual activity may be resumed 2-3 weeks after surgery when it does not feel uncomfortable. 
The skin sutures typically dissolve within 7-10 days after surgery. Patients should be advised to seek immediate medical care for any signs of infection or complications including fever, chills, increasing swelling, excessive bleeding, wound redness, or pus discharge.
Despite successful detorsion, testicular atrophy may occur in some patients. Fertility problems affect up to 40% of patients after testicular torsion.

General

Medical expert: Petrus Järvinen (urologist)

Names of procedure: Scrotal exploration, Testicular detorsion, Testicular torsion surgery, Spermatic cord detorsion, Twisted testicle surgery, Orchiopexy, Testicular fixation 



Goal of operation

To prevent irreversible ischemic injury by restoring testicular perfusion via spermatic cord detorsion and to prevent recurrence through bilateral orchiopexy.



Problem

Testicular torsion that twists the spermatic cord, compressing the testicular artery and venous drainage, leading to ischemia and potential necrosis.

Diagnosis

Torsion of testis (ICD-10: N44.00)  

Short Pathophysiological Description

Testicular torsion is a urological emergency requiring immediate surgical treatment to prevent ischemia and preserve fertility. It occurs when the testicle rotates around the spermatic cord causing compression of the testicular artery, reducing arterial inflow and creating tissue ischemia. Venous drainage is also impaired due to compression of the venous plexus within the twisted cord. Without prompt intervention, the lack of oxygenated blood leads to cellular hypoxia and metabolic dysfunction. If ischemia persists beyond 6 hours, irreversible tissue necrosis begins to occur. The degree of rotation (180 - 720 degrees) and tightness of the twist determines the severity of vascular compromise. Complete arterial occlusion results in rapid onset of severe ischemia, while partial compression may cause gradual deterioration.

The bell clapper deformity is a failure of normal posterior anchoring of the gubernaculum, epididymis and testis. This allows the testicle to rotate more freely on the spermatic cord and increases the risk of torsion. Other risk factors include testicular tumors or prior trauma. However, most patients have no identifiable precipitating cause.



Key Anatomical Structures

Raphe of scrotum

Scrotal wall layers

Skin

Dartos muscle and fascia

External spermatic fascia

Cremasteric muscle and fascia

Internal spermatic fascia

Tunica vaginalis (parietal layer)

Tunica albuginea covering testicular surface

Spermatic cord

Testicular artery

Testicular veins (pampiniform plexus)

Vas deferens

Artery to vas deferens

Testicular nerves

Testis and epididymis 

Appendix testis, appendix epididymis

Pitfalls

Delay of diagnosis or surgery 

Postponing surgery, for example while waiting for ultrasound evaluation, may result in ischemic damage to the testis and lead to testicular atrophy and infertility. When testicular torsion is suspected clinically, immediate surgical exploration should be performed rather than delaying for imaging studies, as time is critical for testicular salvage.





Incorrect evaluation of testicular viability

Accurate assessment of testicular viability is critical to avoid inappropriate orchiectomy. After detorsion, viability is evaluated by observing color return and warming the testicle with warm sponges several minutes. A small test incision may be made to assess for bleeding. Orchiectomy should be reserved for cases of obvious necrosis with no possibility of recovery, as preservation should be attempted whenever there is any doubt about viability. However, leaving definitively necrotic tissue in place risks complications including prolonged post-operative pain and tenderness and possible infection complications.

Incomplete detrosion

Incomplete detorsion may result in fixing the testicle to the scrotal wall while it remains partially twisted, leading to persistent ischemia and testicular atrophy or necrosis.

   

Unilateral fixation only in cases of torsion

Unilateral orchiopexy after confirmed torsion is inadequate because the anatomical predisposition typically affects both testicles. The contralateral testicle remains at high risk for future torsion if not surgically fixed. Bilateral orchiopexy is the standard of care if torsion is confirmed.

Inadequate testicular fixation

Fixation should not be performed with only a single suture, as it may be insufficient to prevent future testicular torsion. The testicle may rotate around a single fixation point, negating the preventive effect of orchiopexy. Multiple sutures should be placed to secure the testicle effectively and prevent recurrent torsion.

Careless tissue handling

Careless dissection through scrotal wall layers may cause inadvertent injury to the underlying testicular surface or tunica albuginea. 

Excessive depth when placing orchiopexy sutures can damage testicular parenchyma or incorporate vital structures within the spermatic cord. 

Uncareful suturing may result in accidental incorporation of testicular vessels compromising blood supply, vas deferens obstruction, or nerve entrapment causing chronic pain.

Insufficient hemostasis

Insufficient hemostasis may result in postoperative bleeding and scrotal hematoma formation, potentially requiring surgical evacuation.

Complications

Testicular atrophy or ischemic necrosis

Incomplete detorsion may result in fixing the testicle to the scrotal wall while still twisted, which can cause testicular atrophy or ischemic necrosis. Proper detorsion can be confirmed by examining the spermatic cord to ensure that all twists have been eliminated. If testicular orientation seems uncertain, comparison with the contralateral testicle, which must be opened anyway for bilateral fixation, can help confirm proper positioning.

Injury to testicular artery

Accidental suturing of the testicular artery may result in compromising testicular blood supply. 

Incautious handling of the spermatic cord may result in damage to the testicular artery, which can lead to bleeding, hematoma formation or even testicular ischemia. 

  

Injury to vas deferens

Uncareful suturing may result in accidental blockage of the vas deference leading to vasal block. 

Incautious handling of the spermatic cord may result in accidental division or damage to the vas, which can lead to necrosis, sperm leakage, or granuloma.

Injury to the pampiniform plexus 

Incautious handling of the spermatic cord may result in damage to the pampiniform plexus, which can lead to bleeding or hematoma formation. 

 

Infection

Surgical site infection is a recognized risk in scrotal procedures. Simple wound infections typically respond to antibiotic therapy and wound care, including suture removal and wound opening if needed. However, retention of necrotic testicular tissue due to incorrect viability assessment can result in severe infectious complications requiring immediate antimicrobial therapy and urgent surgical debridement. Untreated severe infections may progress to life-threatening conditions including sepsis, septic shock, or Fournier's gangrene.

title: Quadriceps tendon repair
secondary_keywords: quadriceps tendon rupture repair steps; extensor mechanism repair knee; quadriceps tendon surgical procedure; transosseous quadriceps tendon repair; quadriceps tendon operative technique
all_keywords: quadriceps tendon repair technique; quadriceps tendon rupture repair steps; extensor mechanism repair knee; quadriceps tendon surgical procedure; transosseous quadriceps tendon repair; quadriceps tendon operative technique

source_knowledge:
Videosurgeon: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)
StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparation Patient is in supine position.
Spinal or general anesthesia can be used.
We recommend placing the knee in slight flexion. This is helpful when drilling the transpatellar tunnels for the sutures. Too much flexion will complicate the tendon repair because of extra tension. 

Planning of the skin incisionIn the transpatellar technique the skin incision is placed anteriorly in the midline. The incision should be long enough to allow suturing of the quadriceps tendon as well as drilling the K-wires through the patella.

Palpate and mark the edges of the patella and the suspected level of the rupture. Use a soft marker, not a pen to avoid breaking the surface of the skin.

The incision should extend a few centimeters below the distal border of the patella, to leave room for the drilling of the K-wires, as well as 5–10 cm above the proximal edge of the patella to allow reliable suture bites to be taken of the quadriceps.

Skin incision and exposure of the rupture site Incise the skin with a scalpel longitudinally as planned. There are no critical structures directly underneath the skin. 

Advance through the prepatellar subcutaneous and bursal tissue to the patellar surface. Make sure to cauterize bleeding vessels.

Expose the patellar tendon distally and the torn quadriceps tendon proximally. Also, expose the vastus medialis muscle belly medially and the vastus lateralis muscle belly laterally. The tendon rupture site is most commonly right next to the superior border of the patella.



Explore the site of the injury. Most of the time the tear extends from the medial to the lateral side from the vastus medialis insertion to the vastus lateralis insertion causing a significant retraction of the entire quadriceps tendon. 

However, sometimes an isolated tear of the rectus femoris tendon can be observed and the tendon is retracted only in the midline. Nevertheless, this is still a tendon rupture which should be recognized and treated early on to maintain the optimal extension of the knee. 

Reattachment of the tendon – quadriceps side The goal is to have two separate sutures on each side of the quadriceps tendon. Ends of the sutures are then pulled through the bony tunnels drilled through the patella and tied together on the distal side of patella. The entry and exit points of the two tendon sutures should be planned in line with the upcoming transpatellar tunnels. 

To start the suturing first place a retractor securely to have better exposure to the whole rupture site.

There are several techniques we can use for the tendon sutures, for example, Krackow, Kessler and Bunnell sutures. Here we use Krackow sutures. 

Sutures are placed by advancing about 5 cm proximally from the rupture site and returning in the distal direction along the midline of the tendon. A braided nonabsorbable suture (e.g., size 5 FiberWire) is used. We start the first tendon suture slightly medial in comparison to the lateral edge of the patella. Keep in mind that the lateral/medial tunnel should not be placed too close to bone margin as not to risk fracture of the patella.

Make sure to pass the sutures through the entire tendon thickness for maximum hold and to leave enough suture thread distally to account for the transpatellar attachment. 

We advance about 5 cm proximally from the rupture site and the return in the distal direction along the midline of the tendon.

The medial suture is done the same way by starting slightly lateral to the medial edge of the patella and exiting in the midline of the tendon.

Reattachment of the tendon – patellar side The goal is to create three longitudinal drill holes in the patella for the sutures to pass through and tie together on distal side of the patella. This method aims to ensure a reliable attachment of the quadriceps tendon to the patella.

Prepare the attachment site by debriding the superior border of the patella for a more secure reattachment of the tendon. You can do this with a rongeur for example.

Plan sites for three longitudinal bone tunnels in the patella. For the middle tunnel the patellar ridge can be used as a landmark to find the midline. The lateral and medial tunnels should not be placed too close to the bone edge as not to cause a fracture of the patella.

The guidewire is drilled from proximal to distal direction. To bring the sutures through the patella use a guide wire with a loop on the other end. Make sure not to perforate the articular surface but stay in the middle between the anterior cortex and the patella's dorsal articular surface.

Also take care not to damage the skin on the proximal tibia and consider using an instrument, for example a chisel, a Semb rasp or a Langenback retractor, to protect the skin from the tip of the guide wire. 

After drilling through the patella from the proximal to the distal end, place the suture in the loop/eyelet and finally pull the guide wire with the now attached threads retrogradely back through the patella. Use oscillating mode to avoid twisting the sutures.

Bring one thread from both the lateral and medial tunnels and two threads from the central tunnel. Make sure that the threads in the middle portion of the tendon are brought through the central tunnel and the lateral and medial threads are brought through the respective tunnels. This prevents twisting of the tendon.

Tie the sutures securely at the distal side of the patella. Make sure to tie multiple (5-7) knots so as to ensure the attachment will not fail. Keeping the lateral and medial sutures separate will prevent twisting the tendon.

Make sure that the distal end of the quadriceps tendon is firmly attached to the proximal edge of the patella so the tension on the tendon is optimal. Sometimes you need to perform a proximal tissue release to help pull the distal end of the tendon to the proximal end of the patella.

Suturing the lateral and medial structures Often, the rupture extends to lateral and medial capsule of the knee. If so, close the capsular tear to achieve watertight closure of the joint.

Using for instance size 2 non-absorbable sutures, begin the closure from the very edge of the rupture line and proceed using continuous or interrupted sutures to the other border of the rupture line.

Cut the ends of threads and test that the sutures are firm enough by bringing the knee into flexion before closing the wound.

Closing the wound The wound closure is done in three layers: two for the subcutis and one for the skin.

The deep subcutaneous layer is closed using absorbable size 0 sutures. Either interrupted or continuous sutures can be used.

The prepatellar bursal tissue provides firm place for deep subcutaneus sutures and enables secure closure of the deeper part of the wound.

The superficial subcutaneus layer is closed by using size 2-0 absorbable continuous sutures. 

The skin is closed using single or continuous sutures or with skin staples.

AftercareGeneral guidelines: There are often local protocols for the postoperative treatment of quadriceps tendon repair. Generally, weight-bearing with an extended knee is allowed. During the first 6 weeks, weight bearing on a flexed knee is not recommended. After 6 weeks, extension exercises are started first with the weight of the leg, then gradually increasing the resistance. We recommend protecting the knee with a hinged knee brace with the knee in full extension or a synthetic cylindrical cast. Thromboprophylaxis should be considered after evaluating individual risk factors. Refer your patient to physical therapy to avoid muscle loss on the affected thigh.

General

Medical expert: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)

Name of procedure: Quadriceps tendon repair



Goal of operation

Reattachment of the torn quadriceps tendon to the patella to enable extension of the knee.

Problem

Quadriceps tendon rupture that leads to loss or weakness of knee extension.

Diagnosis

Quadriceps tendon rupture, Ruptura tendinis quadricipitis femoris (ICD10: S76.1) 

Short pathophysiological description

Quadriceps tendon rupture is typically seen in middle-aged individuals. The tendon rupture happens in a sudden jump or unexpected loading of a partially flexed knee. Natural degeneration of the tendon with aging makes the tendon prone to injuries. The rupture is typically located at the superior border of the patella. Diagnosis of quadriceps tendon rupture is not always easy. Delay of the diagnosis may cause difficulties in the reattachment of the tendon to patella. 

Key anatomical structures 

Quadriceps tendon 

Patella 

Patellar tendon 

Vastus medialis muscle

Vastus lateralis muscle 

Pitfalls

Misplacement of the patellar tunnels 

If the tunnels are drilled too medially or laterally, they might cause a fracture in the patella. If placed too anteriorly there might be cut out through the anterior cortex and in turn, if drilled too dorsally, they might damage the patellar cartilage. 

Problems with sutures

If the sutures are not passed through all the tendon layers a sufficient grip might not be achieved which might lead to failure of reattachment and a re-rupture. 

Insufficient tightening of the knots might lead to retraction of the tendon from the superficial border of the patella. Use at least 5 tight knots to prevent loosening of the fixation. 

Insufficient closure of the capsule might cause synovial fluid to leak to the soft tissue. 

Mobilizing too early 

It is important to educate your patient and tell them to avoid weight-bearing on a flexed knee as it might cause a failure of the tendon repair. Use a brace or a cylindrical cast to protect the repair. We recommend following local rehabilitation protocol and careful consideration of thromboprofylaxis.

Complications

Fracture of the patella

Tunnels drilled too close to the bone margin might cause the patella to fracture.

Rerupture of the tendon

Early weight-bearing on flexed knee within 6 weeks of surgery predisposes to failure of the repair. 

Resisted extension training with external weight should generally start 3 months after the surgery and maximum extension force should be applied only after 9 months. 

Wound healing problems 

Careless soft tissue handling and patient-related risk factors predispose to wound healing problems and can lead to severe infections.

title: Posterior humerus approach
secondary_keywords: posterior approach to humerus; humeral shaft posterior surgical approach; posterior arm approach anatomy; triceps splitting humerus approach; posterior humerus exposure steps
all_keywords: posterior humerus approach technique; posterior approach to humerus; humeral shaft posterior surgical approach; posterior arm approach anatomy; triceps splitting humerus approach; posterior humerus exposure steps
source_knowledge_excerpt: VideoSurgeon: Robert Björkenheim (orthopedic surgeon)  StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparationsThe patient lies prone with the affected arm raised laterally. Alternatively lateral or semi-prone positioning can be used but prone positioning facilitates easier use of fluoroscopy.  The procedure is usually performed under general anesthesia.  Landmarks and incision site The skin incision site should allow sufficient exposure of the fracture site for reduction and fixation. With the posterior approach, the exposure can be achieved particularly to treat middle and distal diaphyseal...
source_knowledge:
VideoSurgeon: Robert Björkenheim (orthopedic surgeon) 
StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparationsThe patient lies prone with the affected arm raised laterally. Alternatively lateral or semi-prone positioning can be used but prone positioning facilitates easier use of fluoroscopy.

The procedure is usually performed under general anesthesia.

Landmarks and incision site The skin incision site should allow sufficient exposure of the fracture site for reduction and fixation. With the posterior approach, the exposure can be achieved particularly to treat middle and distal diaphyseal fractures and distal humeral fractures. 

In practice, the exposure possible to achieve starts from the level of the deltoid muscle and extends to the humero-ulnar joint level. The exact extent of the exposure needed depends on the fracture's location, fixation method and the length of the plate used.

Surrounding neurovascular structures need to be preserved during the procedure.

The skin incision is placed in the middle of posterior arm, using the epicondyles, the humeral shaft and the olecranon as landmarks.

The incision can start from the level of the deltoid muscle inferior border and extend in line with the humeral shaft towards the olecranon. Distally, the incision can curve towards lateral epicondyle for additional exposure.

For uncomplicated healing, the incision is made curving to avoid the olecranon. The curve is made laterally as the ulnar nerve runs medially.

Skin incisionThe skin is incised with a scalpel, following the planned line, down to the subcutaneous fat. There are no critical structures directly underneath the skin.

The incision progresses sharply through the subcutaneous layer along its full length until the brachial fascia. Hemostasis is performed as needed.

The subcutaneous tissue can be slightly mobilized from the fascia surface if needed.

Opening the fasciaUsing scissors the fascia is incised following the placement and length of the skin incision. 

A retractor or retraction sutures can be placed to hold the fascia and subcutaneous layer aside to achieve better visualization.

Once the fascia is opened fully, the long and the lateral head of the triceps along with their joined distal tendinous portion, become visible.

The long and lateral triceps heads overlay the deeper medial head and radial nerve.

Advancing to bone surface lateral to triceps The aim is to achieve exposure to the middle and distal thirds of the posterior humeral diaphysis by retracting the triceps muscle medially, while preserving the radial nerve.
Using the lateral epicondyle as landmark the release of the lateral head of the triceps is started distally with a blunt technique.

In the distal part of upper arm, the radial nerve runs in the anterior compartment. Proximally, it is found in the posterior compartment, near bone surface, crossing the humerus from medial to lateral. The level at which it pierces the lateral intermuscular septum can vary, thus necessitating careful and blunt advancement. 

The radial nerve is often palpable through the muscle, making it easier to locate.

When the radial nerve is visible, it is carefully exposed. As the nerve is traction-sensitive, caution is needed. The accompanying deep brachial artery should also be preserved.

When the radial nerve is exposed and protected, the humeral bone surface can be safely exposed distally using for example a rasp to detach the medial head of triceps from the bone surface.

Proximal exposure of bone surface can be continued up to the deltoid muscle level.

Here, the posterior humeral shaft is visible from the deltoid muscle level distally to provide extensive exposure to the middle and distal third of the shaft. The radial nerve is preserved and the plate can be tunneled beneath it.

Distal extension laterallyIf the fracture is located distally, the plate typically needs to extend to the level of the lateral epicondyle, and the approach needs to be extended distally.

The incision is extended distally, next to the triceps tendon, to prevent injury to the forearm extensor tendon attachment site or the lateral collateral ligament complex, which attach to the lateral epicondyle.

The distal continuation of the approach is sufficient when the distal humerus bone surface is exposed and there is adequate room to position the plate distally.

Additional medial to triceps approach For distal third fractures, additional medial plate insertion may be needed. The goal of the approach is to safely access the medial epicondyle and the posteromedial side of the distal humerus.
The skin incision is extended a bit further to facilitate the advancement medially.

The skin-subcutis flap is mobilized to provide access to the medial side. 

The mobilization is sufficient when the olecranon is exposed.

The ulnar nerve runs tightly along the surface of the bone in its own groove (ulnar groove, sulcus nervi ulnaris) medial to triceps tendon and can be palpated in that area.


The triceps tendon is retracted laterally and the ulnar nerve exposed with a blunt technique.

The ulnar nerve can be exposed proximally depending on the exposure needed.

Distally, in the sulcus area, the ulnar nerve is surrounded by tighter ligament structures that need to be carefully released in line with the triceps tendon in order to move the nerve medially aside.

When the ulnar nerve is released, it can be gently retracted using for example vessel loops, thereby exposing the bone surface of the medial epicondyle.

With the ulnar nerve protected, a finger or a rasp can be used to make room for the plate on the bone surface.

The bone surface postero-medially is exposed and the ulnar nerve kept protected.

Proximal extension mediallyIf more exposure on the medial side is needed, based on the fracture type, it can be achieved by extending the approach proximally.

The medial side of the triceps muscle can be released from the fascia and the ulnar nerve exposed carefully in the proximal direction.

The ulnar nerve, once secured, can be carefully displaced to reveal the proximal bone surface. Awareness of the radial nerve's path beneath the triceps muscle is important during proximal bone surface exposure, such as with a rasp.

The proximal exposure from the medial side is less extensive due to the limitation set by the radial nerve,

compared to the lateral side exposure (where it can reach up to the deltoid muscle).

Joint accessIf joint access is needed, the ulnar nerve can be moved aside to safely incise the joint capsule.

The structures to avoid damaging in this area are the medial collateral ligament and the forearm flexor tendons insertion site on the medial epicondyle. 

The visibility to the tip of the olecranon and the articular surface of the humerus achived. In cases of intra-articular fractures, an olecranon-osteotomy is usually necessary to gain more extensive exposure of the articular surface.

Triceps split approach The goal is to safely expose the fracture site on the mid or distal diaphysis, by laterally retracting the lateral head to the triceps and medially retracting the long head, while preventing radial nerve damage.
The gap between the lateral and long head of the triceps muscle is palpable proximally and space can be developed bluntly between the heads.

Retractors should be placed carefully, especially on the medial side, not to injure the radial nerve underneath.

A finger can be used to advance with caution, in alignment with the muscle fibers, to the tendinous area of the muscle. The radial nerve is located beneath the muscle layer, crossing medial to lateral in this area.

By blunt dissection, the radial nerve and the accompanying deep brachial artery are carefully exposed.

After securing the radial nerve, the tendinous part of the triceps is incised sharply towards the olecranon.

The bone is exposed by dissecting through the deeper muscle layer distally.

The triceps split approach is completed, enabling access from the level of the deltoid muscle to the olecranon fossa. The radial nerve is visible and intact at the proximal part of the wound. 

ClosureThe muscle layer of the triceps split approach can be closed with, for example, single X-sutures using absorbable suture type (e.g. Vicryl 0 or 1). The closure is started from the tendinous portion.

In the middle part of the wound, it’s important not to take too deep bites to avoid damaging the underlying radial nerve. 

In the proximal muscular region, it's sufficient to approximate the heads of the triceps muscle with a single take.

The fascia is closed using absorbable sutures. The position of the ulnar nerve can be confirmed with extension-flexion tests. It should stay securely in its groove and not disclocate medially.

The subcutis is closed with interrupted or continuous absorbable sutures and the skin can be closed with sutures or staples.

Aftercare General guidelines Postoperative movement and stress limitations should be considered case by case depending on underlying illnesses, patient cooperation and fracture type.

General 

Medical experts: Robert Björkenheim (orthopedic surgeon), Thomas Ibounig (orthopedic surgeon), Lauri Kavaja (orthopedic resident)

Name of procedure: Posterior humerus approach, lateral paratricipital approach, medial tricipital approach, triceps split approach

Goal of operation 

To safely gain adequate posterior exposure of the humeral fracture site for reduction and fixation.

Problem 

Fracture involving the middle or distal part of the humerus.

Diagnosis 

Fracture of the shaft of the humerus (ICD-10: S42.3) 

Short pathophysiological description 

Humeral fractures usually result from incidents such as falls or high-energy twisting.

The resulting fracture can lead to a range of complications, including injury to surrounding soft tissues, blood vessels, and nerves, particularly the radial nerve, which is closely associated with the humerus.

The posterior approach is often preferred for fractures involving the posterior aspect of the humeral shaft or the distal humerus, as it provides good visualization and safe access to these areas.

Key anatomical structures 

Humerus

Lateral and medial epicondyles 

Fossa olecranon

Spiral (radial) groove

Cubital tunnel (ulnar groove)

Ulna

Olecranon

Triceps muscle

lateral head

long head

medial head

Deltoid muscle

Brachial fascia

Lateral and medial intermuscular septum

Radial nerve 

Ulnar nerve

Axillary nerve 

Deep brachial artery

Medial and lateral collateral ligament complex of the elbow

Forearm extensor and flexors attachment sites

Pitfalls

Incorrect site of incision 

If the skin incision is done on the medial side of the olecranon, the ulnar nerve is at risk. A skin incision made over the olecranon leads to more tension on the wound, and might hinder wound healing. 

To avoid these issues, the incision is extended distally on the lateral side of the olecranon. 

Proximally, the skin incision and the approach is limited by the axillary nerve running under the deltoid muscle.

Careless tissue handling 

Excessive tension on the radial and ulnar nerves should be avoided while they are being protected. The radial nerve, in particular, is easily affected by stretching, which may result in temporary paralysis. Gentle handling of the nerves is vital.

These nerves can also be damaged by a scalpel, scissors, or a retractor. Nerve damage usually occurs due to improper visualization. A sufficiently long skin incision can help to operate under direct visibility and avoid nerve damage.

Careless placement of the plate 

Inadequate exposure of the radial nerve and/or careless placement of the plate may result in the plate ending up on the radial nerve in the posterior humeral aspect. When placing the plate, it is vitally important to avoid trapping the radial nerve between the plate and the bone. 

In the area of the medial epicondyle, the ulnar nerve may be trapped if it is not moved aside when the plate is being placed.

Complications 

Radial nerve injury 

The radial nerve is particularly sensitive to stretch injuries, making gentle handling of the nerve vital. If the nerve is not adequately exposed, or if the plate is placed carelessly, the nerve may become compressed under the plate. Throughout the procedure, the course of the radial nerve should always be acknowledged.

Ulnar nerve injury 

At the area of the medial epicondyle, the ulnar nerve runs in a narrow groove. To avoid nerve injury, it is vital to carefully move the nerve aside (medially), if exposure of the medial side is needed.

Vascular complications 

Major vessels run anterior to the humerus, so vascular complications are rare when using the posterior approach to the humerus. In the posterior approach, the plate is placed over the fracture on the posterior aspect of the humerus, and drilling is performed through both cortices of the humerus in the PA direction. Vascular wall injury may occur when drilling the holes for the plate or fastening the screws.

title: Patellar fracture fixation with tension band
secondary_keywords: patella ORIF tension band wiring; patellar fracture fixation steps; tension band fixation patella procedure; patella fracture operative technique; extensor mechanism fracture fixation
all_keywords: patellar fracture tension band technique; patella ORIF tension band wiring; patellar fracture fixation steps; tension band fixation patella procedure; patella fracture operative technique; extensor mechanism fracture fixation
source_knowledge_excerpt: Video Surgeon: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon) Steps Patient positioning, anesthesia and preparation Patient is in a supine position. Either spinal or general anesthesia can be used. source_knowledge:
Video Surgeon: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)
Steps Patient positioning, anesthesia and preparation Patient is in a supine position. 

It’s recommended to place the knee in slight flexion. This facilitates the drilling of the transpatellar K-wires. Excessive flexion will hinder fracture reduction due to extra tension. 

Either spinal or general anesthesia can be used. 

Landmarks and incision site The goal is to plan an incision that ensures optimal exposure of the fracture site for precise reduction and fixation, with consideration for the later insertion of K-wires through the patella.

Mark the edges of the patella and the suspected level of the fracture.

The incision is planned anteriorly in the midline. It should extend a few centimeters below the patella's distal border, and about 5 cm above the patella's proximal border to leave room for the K-wires' drilling. The horizontal markings on the skin will assist with precise skin closure.

Skin incision and exposing the fracture site Exposing the quadriceps tendon, the patella, and the patellar tendon ensures proper visibility and access for the later positioning and insertion of fixation material.
Incise the skin with a scalpel longitudinally as planned. There are no critical structures directly underneath the skin. 

Advance through the subcutaneous and prepatellar bursal tissue to the fascia surface. Cauterize any bleeding vessels.

Use retractors as needed to maintain exposure. In this video the patella is still intact at this point.

Typically the bursal tissue on the medial and lateral side of patella needs to be released to achieve better visualization of the full fracture site.

The exposure is sufficient when the whole patella, along with the proximal part of the patellar tendon and the distal part of the quadriceps tendon, are visible.

Fracture reduction The goal is to restore the normal alignment and shape of the patella, ensuring the evenness of the articular surface for optimal joint function.
Often, an injury can result in a tear in the joint capsule, either on the lateral or medial side of the fracture. This capsular tear can create a 'window' that can be used to assess the fracture reduction.

Remove any small loose fragments from the fracture surface and clear possible hematoma from the fracture site to facilitate anatomic reduction. If necessary, rinse the knee joint with saline.

Use a reduction clamp to secure and maintain the fracture reduction in place.

Confirm the adequacy of the reduction by palpating the evenness of the articular surface through the soft tissue window.

K-wire placement The goal of the K-wire placement is to secure and maintain the correct alignment of the fractured bone fragments, providing a foundation for the subsequent placement of the cerclage wire. The K-wires should be positioned in a way that ensures stability, without being too close to the cortices or joint line.

There are various methods for inserting K-wires into the patella. Here, two K-wires are drilled in a proximal to distal direction across the fracture line.

Position the K-wire approximately 5 mm from the anterior cortex of the patella. The lateral K-wire should be placed longitudinally slightly lateral from the midline and the medial K-wire slightly medial from the midline.

Insert the K-wire through the quadriceps tendon and into the surface of the patella.

Drill the K-wire through the patella. Avoid damaging the skin on the distal side.

Drill a second K-wire parallel to the first one. Leave approximately 1 cm between the two K-wires to provide stability.

Using fluoroscopy, check for proper alignment and reduction of the fracture and verify the correct alignment of the joint line. Assess the symmetry and placement of the K-wires, confirming they are not too close to the cortices or joint line.

Cerglace wire placement The goal is to stabilize the fracture and minimize displacement. This is achieved by placing a cerglace wire in a figure-of-eight configuration beneath the K-wires and as close to the patellar bone surface as possible, providing direct support to the bone structure.
Use a needle to create a horizontal path for the cerclage wire on the proximal aspect of the patella. The tip of the needle can be used to palpate the patellar cortex, aiming to position it as close as possible to the patellar surface.

Ensure that the needle is placed beneath the K-wires.

Pass the cerclage wire through the needle, while simultaneously pulling out the needle. 

Cross the wire ends in the middle to form a figure of eight configuration.

Repeat the insertion of the wire distally, again close to patellar surface and below the K wires.

Connect the wire ends to complete the figure-eight configuration. Leave sufficient wire length to form the loop on the opposite side.

Gradually tighten these loops, alternating between the two sides to ensure balanced compression. The loops should be tightened in opposite directions - one clockwise and the other counterclockwise - for optimal stability.

Using fluoroscopy, asses the tension band wiring from AP and lateral view. Ensure the reduction remains satisfactory and the cerclage wire is correctly positioned under the K-wires. The wire should be close to the bone surface. Adjust the wire's tension if necessary.

Finalizing the fixation Fixation is secured by creating hooks on the ends of the K-wires. These hooks hold both the K-wires and the cerclage wire in place. The aim is to get the K-wire hooks around the tension band wire to prevent it from slipping from the patellar surface.
Bend the proximal ends of the K-wires 180-degrees with pliers to form a hook and cut the excess part. 

The hook is turned posteriorly, so make sure that the hook is small enough so that it stays inside the patella when fixed in place.

Make small incisions on the quadriceps tendon for the K-wire hooks.

Rotate the hooks 180-degrees and tap the hooks into the patella through the small incisions.

Cut the excess parts of the K-wires distally. Cut them close to the patellar tendon so that distal ends do not poke the skin after closure. A subtle bending of the distal ends of the K-wires can be used to prevent inadvent retraction of the K-wires. However, excessive bending makes it difficult to remove the K-wires later if needed to. 

Bend the ends of the twisted cerclage wires into the soft tissue to prevent soft tissue irritation. If necessary, cut any excessive wire.

Assess the fixation with fluoroscopy. The bent K-wires should go around the cerclage wire and the wire should run on the patellar surface (or as close as possible).

Lastly, examine the stability of the fixation. Flex the knee and check that the fracture line is flush, and no separation is detected.

ClosureUse size 2 absorbable sutures to repair the torn capsule, ensuring a watertight joint capsule.

The subcutaneous layer is closed using absorbable size 0 sutures on the deeper layer and size 2-0 sutures on the superficial layer. Either interrupted or continuous sutures can be used.

The skin is closed using single or continuous sutures or with staples.

Aftercare General guidelines:There are often local protocols for the postoperative treatment of patellar fracture.

Generally, weight-bearing with an extended knee is allowed. During the first 6 weeks, weight bearing on a flexed knee is not recommended. After 6 weeks, extension exercises are started first with the weight of the leg, then gradually increasing the resistance. 

We recommend protecting the knee with a hinged knee brace with the knee in extension or a synthetic cylindrical cast. 

Thromboprophylaxis should be considered after evaluating individual risk factors. 

Sometimes the extension band causes irritation, and the fixation material needs to be removed. If this is the case, you need to first ensure that the fracture has healed properly before taking out the fixation material.

General 

Medical experts: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)

Name of Procedure: Patellar fracture fixation with tension band, Tension band wiring (TBW) of the patella, Cerglace compression wiring.

Goal of Operation 

Reduction of the patellar joint surface and stable fracture fixation to enable healing of the extensor mechanism of the knee.

Problem 

Reduced stability and mobility of the knee, leading to impaired knee extension caused by patellar fracture.

Diagnosis 

Patellar fracture, fractura patellae (ICD-10: S82.0) 

Short Pathophysiological Description

The patella is attached to the quadriceps tendon and aids in the extension of the leg by increasing the leverage of the quadriceps tendon. A patellar fracture disrupts this mechanism leading to impaired extension mechanism. The fracture is usually caused by direct trauma, such as a fall onto the knee or a sharp blow in a car accident. 

The patella can be fractured either transverse or to multiple fragments. If the fracture is displaced, it might not heal properly. This could result in chronic pain, instability, and loss of knee function. Repairing the fracture surgically and restoring the extensor mechanism can improve the prognosis. If not treated properly, it could lead to osteoarthritis due to abnormal stress on the joint. 

Key anatomical structures 

Patella 

Patellar tendon 

Quadriceps tendon 

Knee joint capsule

Pitfalls

Misplacement of the K-wires 

Too anterior placement of the K-wires may cause iatrogenic fracture of the anterior cortex of the patella leading to an unstable fixation. Too posterior placement may cause iatrogenic injury to the patellar articular surface. 

Place the K-wires approximately 5 mm from the anterior surface to avoid these injuries. 

Misplacement of the cerclage wire 

If the cerclage wire is not placed under the K-wires at the distal and proximal pole of the patella, or there is no proper tensioning of the wire, this may lead to loss of reduction. 

Use fluoroscopy to confirm the correct location of the cerclage wire, the proper tension of the band, and the proper fixation of the tension band with the K-wire hooks. 

Mobilizing too early 

Patients should avoid weight-bearing on a flexed knee in the first 6 weeks as it might cause a failure of the repair. Use a brace or a cylindrical cast to protect the repair. We recommend following local rehabilitation protocol.

Complications 

Loss of reduction 

Early weight-bearing on flexed knee within 6 weeks of surgery predisposes to failure of the repair.

Resisted extension training with external weight should be generally started 3 months after the surgery and maximal extension force should be applied only after 9 months. 

Also, incorrect placement of the tension band may lead to loss of reduction. 

Wound healing problems 

Careless soft tissue handling and patient-related risk factors predispose wound healing problems and lead sometimes to severe infections.

title: Olecranon fracture - tension band
