The lower limb is sterilely prepped and draped, leaving a window from the hip joint level to the ankle joint.   Insertion of tibial pins Palpate the anteromedial surface of the tibia.  In proximal tibia fractures, be sure to consider the definitive osteosynthesis operation and plan the...
source_knowledge:
Video Surgeon: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)

StepsPatient positioning, preparation and anesthesia The patient is positioned supine on the operating table.
Both general anesthesia and spinal anesthesia can be used. If chosen, spinal anesthesia should be used carefully so as to not mask a compartment syndrome.
The lower limb is sterilely prepped and draped, leaving a window from the hip joint level to the ankle joint. 

Insertion of tibial pins Palpate the anteromedial surface of the tibia.

In proximal tibia fractures, be sure to consider the definitive osteosynthesis operation and plan the pin placement distal enough so that the future internal fixation hardware will not involve the pin site.
Make a small stab incision in the skin and bluntly prepare a route to the bone surface.

Place the drill guide through the route you just prepared and hold it securely against the anteromedial tibial bone.
Drill a hole in the anterior cortex through the drill guide. If you have self-drilling pins, maintain the position of the drill guide and remove the drill and inner sleeve. In the absence of self-drilling pins, drill a hole in the contralateral cortex as well before removing the drill and inner sleeve. 

Advance the half pin all the way to the far cortex by hand or carefully by a power tool. It may be difficult to appreciate the pin advancing through the far cortex and so use fluoroscopy while you are advancing.

Confirm that the pin is through the contralateral cortex using fluoroscopy. Bicortical fixation is a prerequisite for secure pin anchoring. Remove the drill guide 

For placement of the second tibial pin, use the parallel drill guide with two soft tissue protectors. One of the soft tissue protectors will be slid over the half pin that you just placed and the other will mark the site of the second pin. This is necessary so that the second half pin will be placed at an appropriate distance and parallel with the first one. This ensures that the pin clamp will be able to appropriately be placed between the two pins.
Place the second half pin using the same procedure as you did for the first one.
Connect the pins with a pin clamp.

Insertion of femoral pins Palpate the femoral diaphysis. 

Envision the pin placement in the proximal-distal direction in a manner such that they are not in the region of future fracture fixation. In the axial plane, the pins will be placed laterally or anterolaterally. 
Make a small stab incision in the skin and bluntly prepare a route to the bone surface. 

Place the drill guide through the route you just prepared and hold it securely against the anterolateral femoral bone.

Drill a hole in the anterolateral cortex via the drill guide. If you have self-drilling pins, maintain the position of the drill guide and remove the drill and inner sleeve. In the absence of self-drilling pins, drill a hole in the contralateral cortex as well before removing the drill and inner sleeve. 

Advance the half pin all the way to the far cortex by hand or carefully by a power tool. It may be difficult to appreciate the pin advancing through the far cortex and so use fluoroscopy while you are advancing. 

Confirm that the pin is through the contralateral cortex using fluoroscopy. Bicortical fixation is a prerequisite for secure pin anchoring. Remove the drill guide. 

For placement of the second femoral pin, use the parallel drill guide with two soft tissue protectors. One of the soft tissue protectors will be slide over the half pin that you just placed and the other will mark the site of the second pin. This is necessary so that the second half pin will be placed at an appropriate distance and parallel with the first one. This ensures that the pin clamp will be able to appropriately be placed between the two pins. 
Place the second half pin using the same procedure as you did for the first one
Connect the pins with a pin clamp.

Fracture reduction and frame construction Use posts in the pin clamp, and rod-to-rod couplers to place the long rods between the pins. Do not tighten the rods fully at this stage. 

Reduce the fracture or the knee joint. 

Tighten the rod-to-rod connectors.

Check the fracture position or the knee posture by fluoroscopy.
Readjust the position of the leg if necessary to make sure that the limb length, alignment, and rotation are acceptable 

Protect the pin sites with wound dressings.This can include a xeroform on the pin site followed by a halfway-cut gauze with a kerlix dressing wrapped around the pins to keep the dressing in place. 

AftercareGeneral guidelines:Elevate the leg slightly using pillows. The vitality of the limb should be monitored carefully. Knee subluxations/dislocations are associated with a risk of injury to the popliteal artery and proximal tibial fractures are associated with a risk of compartment syndrome. 
Once the soft tissue status has improved and the swelling subsided, the external fixation can be removed, and internal fixation can be performed.

General

Medical experts: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)

Name of Procedure: Knee spanning external fixation



Goal of operation

Temporary fixation of a high-energy injury in the knee region (e.g. high energy fracture of the distal femur or proximal tibia, knee subluxation/dislocation) until the soft tissue status allows for internal fracture fixation or ligament repair.

Problem

Fracture of the distal femur, fracture of the proximal tibia, knee subluxation/dislocation 

Diagnosis

Fracture of lower end of femur (ICD10: S72.4) 

Fracture of upper end of tibia (ICD10: S82.1) 

Subluxation and dislocation of knee (ICD10: S83.1) 

Short Pathophysiological Description

High energy distal femoral fractures, high energy proximal tibial fractures and/or knee subluxation/dislocation often results in significant soft tissue swelling, preventing initial internal fixation. If the fracture reduction (limb length, alignment and rotation) cannot be supported by splinting/casting, the limb should be supported temporarily via external fixation. 

Key Anatomical Structures

Femur

Tibia

Knee joint

Deep femoral artery

Posterior tibial artery

Tibial nerve

Pitfalls 

Incorrect pin position

If a pin is placed too close to the bone margin and not centrally in the bone, there is a risk of pin failure and/or stress riser formation resulting in an iatrogenic fracture. 

If the pin does not fully penetrate the far cortex, it may loosen. 

If the tibial pins are placed too close to the tibial fracture line, the fixation plate to be placed later will be in a contaminated area. 

Technical challenges or lack of understanding biomechanics 

Technical challenges include achieving appropriate fracture reduction (e.g. restoring length, alignment and rotation). Failure to respect the biomechanical principles of external fixation may lead to a frame that does not stabilize the fracture properly and/or a frame that may fail. The manual of the manufacturer of this external fixator can be found here. 

Careless tissue handling 

After the skin incision, it is advisable to proceed bluntly to the bone surface in order to avoid unnecessary soft tissue injury, for example to cutaneous nerve branches or superficial vascular structures. 

If the pin is advanced too far past the opposite cortex, it may cause injury to soft tissue structures on the opposite side of the bone. 

The pin-clamp should be placed relatively close to the skin in order to maximize construct stability, but neither the connector nor any frame structures should be in contact with the skin because there needs to be room for additional soft tissue swelling.

Complications

Injuries to neurovascular structures 

Structures located posterior to tibia (e.g. posterior tibial artery, tibial nerve) or medial to femur (e.g. deep femoral artery) may be injured if the pin is advanced too far or in the wrong direction. The correct direction is from anteromedial to posterolateral in the tibia and from anterolateral to posteromedial in the femur. The correct depth of the pin (just through the opposite cortex) should be confirmed with fluoroscopy. 

Fixation failure 

If a pin is placed too close to the bone margin and not centrally in the bone, there is an increased risk of pin failure or iatrogenic fracture. 

If the pin does not penetrate through the opposite cortex properly, it may lose purchase in the bone. 

If the rod clamps are not tightened properly, the fixation will be unstable and the fracture reduction may be lost. 

Infections 

Pins placed too close to the fracture area may make the area prone to infection during the subsequent osteosynthesis operation, as there is a direct route from the skin to the bone surface.

title: Knee joint injection
secondary_keywords: intra articular knee injection procedure; knee corticosteroid injection landmarks; knee aspiration and injection steps; orthopedic knee injection approach; knee joint injection step by step
all_keywords: knee joint injection technique; intra articular knee injection procedure; knee corticosteroid injection landmarks; knee aspiration and injection steps; orthopedic knee injection approach; knee joint injection step by step
source_knowledge_excerpt: VideoDoctor: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist) StepsPreparations and patient positioningThe patient is in a supine position with knee extended and the skin of the legs exposed.   Select the equipment: syringesneedle: blue 23G, green 21G or pink 18G  any medicinal agent to be injected, according to the indication For large joints, preferably Methylprednisolone 40 mg/ml, for example 80 mg (2 ml) or Triamcinolone 20 mg/ml, for example 20 mg (1 ml) Dilution of glucocorticoids with saline, or with local anesthetic is advisable when injecting into large joints as dilution has been found to reduce...
source_knowledge:
VideoDoctor: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist)
StepsPreparations and patient positioningThe patient is in a supine position with knee extended and the skin of the legs exposed. 

Select the equipment: syringesneedle: blue 23G, green 21G or pink 18G

any medicinal agent to be injected, according to the indication For large joints, preferably Methylprednisolone 40 mg/ml, for example 80 mg (2 ml) or Triamcinolone 20 mg/ml, for example 20 mg (1 ml) Dilution of glucocorticoids with saline, or with local anesthetic is advisable when injecting into large joints as dilution has been found to reduce the tendency to pain after the injection. A diluted glucocorticoid is also distributed more evenly in the joint. For the knee joint, the dilution ratio for glucocorticoids can be 1:2-4, for example. Fixed combinations of glucocorticoid with an anesthetic can also be used. 

Landmarks and injection sitePalpate the superior lateral edge of the patella. A good injection site is about 0.5 cm below it in the AP direction.



Mark the injection site. 

Clean the skin area.

InjectionAdvance from the marked injection site through the skin with the needle horizontally.

Aspiration of the synovial fluid is best done at a depth of approximately 2 cm.

Synovial fluid analysisThe turbidity and color of the synovial fluid can be assessed visually. Fairly clear synovial fluid with visible graduation scale is a sign of no pronounced infection. Cloudy synovial fluid is caused by large number of white blood cells making the graduation scale invisible.

In unexplained fluid accumulation, take a specimen if possible. 
- Cell count + crystal analysis and bacterial culture to be ordered - If antibiotic therapy has already been started and there is strong suspicion of septic arthritis, bacterial nucleic acid detection to be ordered - If Lyme disease-induced arthropathy is suspected, Borrelia nucleic acid detection to be ordered - You can also take a swab sample for bacterial culture if the synovial fluid yield is small. 

Injecting the medicinal agent if indicatedAfter evacuation of the knee joint you can use the same needle for the intra-articular injection as indicated up to the point of low resistance.

AftercareGeneral guidelines:The puncture site should be covered by an adhesive patch and kept dry and clean for 24 hours to minimize the risk of infection. 
For a better therapeutic effect, avoid stress of the punctured joint for 24-48 hours and avoid heavy strain for one week.
Glucocorticoids are more absorbed into the bloodstream, particularly from large weight-bearing joints, if the joint is stressed.

General 

Medical expert: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist)

Names of procedure: Knee joint injection, knee joint puncture, knee arthrocentesis, intra-articular injection of the knee, knee joint aspiration

Goal of Operation

Safe knee joint puncture for synovial fluid aspiration and/or to administer local therapeutic injection. 

Problem

Unknown etiology of a knee joint effusion. The need for local treatment of knee osteoarthritis or inflammatory condition. 

Diagnosis

Osteoarthritis of knee (ICD-10: M17.9) 

Arthritis, unspecified (ICD-10: M13.9) 

Other rheumatoid arthritis with rheumatoid factor (ICD-10: M05.8) 

Reactive arthropathy (ICD-10: M02.9) 

Idiopathic gout (ICD-10: M10.0) 

Other chondrocalcinosis (ICD-10: M11.2) 

Pyogenic arthritis (ICD-10: M00.9) 



Short Pathophysiological Description

The most common cause of fluid accumulation in the knee joint is irritation due to mechanical stress. In an intact knee joint, there is a small quantity of synovial fluid produced in the joint capsule to act as lubricant; it also has a nutritional effect on the joint. 


Osteoarthritis, degenerative meniscus lesions, knee joint injuries and overload (e.g. from jogging lengthy distances) may result in excess fluid accumulation in the joint. In rheumatic inflammatory arthropathies, autoimmune inflammation will make the synovial capsule produce large quantities of inflammatory effusion fluid in the joint. In crystal-induced arthritis, the synovial membrane is irritated by crystals of sodium urate or calcium pyrophosphate formed in the joint. 

Key anatomical structures

Patella 

Femur 

Quadriceps femoris 

Joint capsule of the knee 

Suprapatellar joint recess 

Pitfalls 

Wrong injection site

Before intra-articular injection of the medicinal agent make sure the needle is in the synovial cavity or synovial recess by synovial fluid aspiration. 

Challenges with sample aspiration 

If the synovial capsule is thickened, the villous synovial proliferations/synovial debris may block the needle during aspiration of synovial fluid. The stronger the aspiration and the larger the syringe, the greater the risk. Try changing to a smaller syringe. Try clearing the blocked needle tip by injecting a small quantity of fluid into the joint and then re-aspirate. 

If sample aspiration is unsuccessful when acute gout is suspected, try saline or heparin irrigation. After injecting 1 ml of fluid try aspirating again. This will also yield a sample for bacterial analysis. 



Storage of the synovial fluid sample 

A synovial fluid sample should be analyzed promptly after taking the sample. Synovial fluid containing blood will rapidly coagulate. There may be cell degradation in the sample after just an hour. Crystals, on the other hand, are inorganic salts and are better preserved and so their analysis can be made the next day.

Injecting too frequently 

In case of acute inflammation, intra-articular injections can be given at 1-month intervals for the first 3 months. Afterwards, if needed, 3 months apart.

There is no strong evidence of adverse effects of glucocorticoids on the joint, although frequent injections may cause unwanted systemic effects.

Complications

Infection 

Intra-articular infection after a joint injection is very rare, the incidence being 1:2000-1:100 000, most commonly 1:10 000. Nevertheless, maintain aseptic technique while doing the procedure. 



Joint pain 

Joint pain during 24 hours post-injection is an adverse effect occurring in up to 10% of cases, particularly with glucocorticoid injections. It may result from a local tissue injury caused by the needle alone but is usually due to an irritation reaction to the crystalline glucocorticoid or hyaluronate. The joint may be painful, perhaps even feel warm, for about 24 hours, but these symptoms ease spontaneously. The symptoms can be alleviated by analgesics or cold treatment. 



Hemarthrosis 

Intra-articular hemorrhage is a very rare complication of a joint injection. Antithrombotic treatment or anticoagulant medication is not a contraindication for intra-articular injection as the risk is very small, about 0-2%, even in patients using these medicines. In patients on anticoagulants, however, the smallest possible needle is recommended for joint injection. 



Local skin atrophy or hypopigmentation 

Local skin atrophy occurs in about 1% of the patients after intra-articular injection of glucocorticoids. Typically, atrophy at the injection area develops at 1-4 months post-injection. In addition to atrophy, there may be hypopigmentation around the injection area. Atrophy and hypopigmentation are caused by subcutaneous glucocorticoid accumulation, potentially due to extracapsular injection or extracapsular seepage of the glucocorticoid via the puncture canal. 



Tendon tear 

Tendon tears during intra-articular injections are very rare and are often due to unintentional injection of glucocorticoid into the adjacent tendon rather than the joint itself. To avoid tendon injuries, glucocorticoids should always be injected against low resistance. 



Pericapsular calcium deposit 

Pericapsular calcification, or calcium deposits around the joint capsule of the target joint is a very common adverse effect, occurring after intra-articular injections in up to 10% of cases. Pericapsular calcium deposits are usually asymptomatic and harmless. In most cases, calcium is spontaneously absorbed from the synovial capsule but in some patients X-ray images may show even permanent calcifications, particularly in small joints such as the PIP and the DIP joints of the fingers. 



Compromise of diabetes management 

Following intra-articular injections, some of the glucocorticoid dose will be absorbed into the systemic bloodstream and in diabetic patients, for example, blood sugar level might be elevated for a few days post-injection. The risk is highest for short-acting agents or when multiple injections are given together. Patients with diabetes should be advised to step up their blood sugar monitoring post-injection, and if necessary to adjust their diabetes medication doses temporarily.

title: Intraosseus (IO) access: proximal tibia and humerus
secondary_keywords: humeral IO access procedure; IO line insertion step by step; proximal tibia intraosseous placement; emergency intraosseous access landmarks; tibial and humeral IO technique
all_keywords: intraosseous access proximal tibia technique; humeral IO access procedure; IO line insertion step by step; proximal tibia intraosseous placement; emergency intraosseous access landmarks; tibial and humeral IO technique
source_knowledge_excerpt: VideoSurgeon: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon) Steps Equipment Select the equipment, such as the commonly used EZ-IO drill with an integrated battery, lasting years in ordinary use. Test that the drill is working correctly.  There are three different needles, color-coded for length.- Yellow 45mm- Blue 25 mm - Pink 15 mm   The needles have black marks for depth at 10 mm intervals, starting 5 mm from the hub. When inserting the needle through the skin and subcutis down to the bone surface, minimally the 5 mm mark should still be visible.   The choice of needle depends on the site of insertion and...
source_knowledge:
VideoSurgeon: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon)
Steps Equipment Select the equipment, such as the commonly used EZ-IO drill with an integrated battery, lasting years in ordinary use. Test that the drill is working correctly.

There are three different needles, color-coded for length.- Yellow 45mm- Blue 25 mm - Pink 15 mm 

The needles have black marks for depth at 10 mm intervals, starting 5 mm from the hub. When inserting the needle through the skin and subcutis down to the bone surface, minimally the 5 mm mark should still be visible. 

The choice of needle depends on the site of insertion and the thickness of the subcutis:
- The yellow needle is suitable for the proximal humerus and the blue needle for the proximal tibia. 
- Less common sites for IO access are the femoral lateral condyle (yellow needle) and the medial malleolus (blue needle). IO access to the sternum requires specialized equipment. 
- If the patient is heavily built or obese, a longer needle can be used (for example a yellow needle can be used for the proximal tibia). 
The needle is attached to the drill with a magnet.

Preparations If the clinical status allows, the patient is informed about the coming procedure and the equipment is tested.
The air is flushed from the tubing.
The chosen area is cleaned and allowed to dry.

Landmarks and insertion technique - tibiaThe patient is in supine position.
The patella and the tibial tuberosity are palpated.

The needle is placed 1-2 cm medial to the tibial tuberosity, perpendicular to the bone. 

Press the needle to the bone surface through the skin. Make sure that at least one black mark of the needle is visible above the skin.

Activate the driver and, at full speed, drill through the cortex until there is loss of bone resistance. This indicates that the needle has passed through the cortical bone into the trabecular bone. 

Hold the hub firmly with one hand, detach the drill by pulling, and rotate the needle to remove it. In most cases a correctly placed IO needle needs no separate fixation but the manufacturer’s fixation equipment should still be used.

Blood samples can be collected via the needle, after which the tubing connected to the needle is flushed with 10 ml of saline.
A conscious patient can be given a slow bolus of lidocaine for analgesia. The IO access is then ready for use.
A successfully placed IO access drains as well as an IV access but a pressure bag can be used if necessary. 

Landmarks and insertion technique - humerusThe humerus is placed in inward rotation and adduction (the patient’s palm on the abdomen and the upper arm against the trunk).
One hand is placed in the patient’s axillary fold (medial edge of the deltoid muscle) and the other on the lateral side of humerus (mid-axillary line). The ball of the humeral head can be felt where the thumbs meet. This site can also be felt by palpating the humerus from the distal direction proximally until the greater tubercle of the humerus is felt.

The aim is to drill the needle towards the midpoint of the humeral head. The drilling angle is 45 degrees to the skin, tilted towards the elbow. This helps to sink the needle into the humeral head and prevents it from sliding along the humerus surface towards the subacromial space.

Press the needle to the bone surface through the skin. Make sure that at least one black mark of the needle is visible above the skin.

Activate the driver and, at full speed, drill through the cortex until there is loss of bone resistance. This indicates that the needle has passed through the cortical bone into the spongy/trabecular bone. 

Hold the hub firmly with one hand, detach the drill by pulling, and rotate the needle to remove it. In most cases a correctly placed IO needle needs no separate fixation but the manufacturer’s fixation equipment should still be used.

Blood samples can be collected via the needle, after which the tubing connected to the needle is flushed with 10 ml of saline.
A conscious patient can be given a slow bolus of lidocaine for analgesia. The IO access is then ready for use.
A successfully placed IO access drains as well as an IV access but a pressure bag can be used if necessary. 

AftercareGeneral guidelinesAn IO access can remain in place for 24 hours. An IV access can typically be inserted within this time. The IO needle is removed by replacing the mandrine and then rotating and pulling the needle out. The puncture wound is covered with a wound dressing.

General

Medical experts: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon)

Name of Procedure: Intraosseus (IO) access: proximal tibia and humerus

Goal of Operation

Establishing IV access via intraosseous line.

Problem

IV access to a peripheral vein fails or would take too long.

Short Pathophysiological Description

Intraosseous (IO) access is an excellent way of establishing vascular access in urgent cases (trauma, burn, shock, resuscitation) when establishing venous access fails or would take too long. All intravenous medications, fluids, and contrast media for imaging can be administered via IO access. 

The preferred site for IO access is the proximal humerus as any fluids given via this route end up in the systemic circulation via the superior vena cava. IO/IV access in lower limbs is not recommended for trauma patients with pelvic or abdominal injuries as due to extravasation the desired response to the medication/fluids given may not be achieved. 

Contraindications: 

Fracture of the limb planned for the insertion 

Potential venous injury proximal to the planned site of IO access insertion 

(Skin) infection at the site of insertion of the IO access 

Osteopetrosis or osteogenesis imperfecta 

Key anatomical structures

Humerus 

humeral head 

surgical humeral neck 

Proximal tibia 

tibial tuberosity 

Pitfalls

Incorrect depth of insertion 

If the needle is not correctly inserted into the spongy bone, it may result in extravasation. This can occur: 

if the IO needle does not penetrate the hard cortical bone, i.e. the depth of insertion is too short. 

The IO needle reaches the opposite cortex, i.e. the insertion depth is too great. 



Also note that the hub of the IO needle should not compresses the skin which could cause local skin necrosis and pain. 

Challenges in interpretating the results 

Notice, that not all lab results collected from IO blood correspond to those of the venous samples. Generally, the hemoglobin and creatinine values correspond to those of the venous samples, whereas the leukocyte, platelet, Ca, Na and K values do not necessarily correspond to those of the venous samples.

Complications

Extravasation

If the tip of the needle is not in the trabecular bone this may lead to extravasation i.e. the infusion will end up in the surrounding soft tissue. 

Advance the needle to the bone surface and make sure that at least one black mark of the needle is visible. Drill through the cortex until there is loss of bone resistance. This indicates that the needle has passed through the cortical bone into the spongy bone. 

Muscular compartment syndrome 

A rare complication associated with extravasation in the lower leg area is acute compartment syndrome. If the infusion is continued despite extravasation, the fluid ending up in the muscular compartments may result in pressure elevation and muscular compartment syndrome.

title: Intramedullary nailing of the tibia
secondary_keywords: tibia IM nail procedure; tibial shaft fracture nailing steps; infrapatellar tibial nail technique; tibial intramedullary fixation operative steps; tibia fracture IM nailing approach
all_keywords: tibial intramedullary nailing technique; tibia IM nail procedure; tibial shaft fracture nailing steps; infrapatellar tibial nail technique; tibial intramedullary fixation operative steps; tibia fracture IM nailing approach
source_knowledge_excerpt: Patient positioning and preparationFracture reduction and incision site planningLandmarks and incisionExposing the nail entry site&placing the guide pinOpening the cortexInserting the guide wireReamingNail insertionProximal locking of the nailDistal locking of the nailClosureSurgeon: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)  StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparationThe patient is positioned supine.   Due to the risk of acute compartment syndrome (ACS), general anesthesia is preferred. Close monitoring of ACS symptoms should be continued after surgery.   Positioning of the fractured leg...
source_knowledge:
Patient positioning and preparationFracture reduction and incision site planningLandmarks and incisionExposing the nail entry site&placing the guide pinOpening the cortexInserting the guide wireReamingNail insertionProximal locking of the nailDistal locking of the nailClosureSurgeon: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)

StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparationThe patient is positioned supine. 

Due to the risk of acute compartment syndrome (ACS), general anesthesia is preferred. Close monitoring of ACS symptoms should be continued after surgery. 

Positioning of the fractured leg depends on local protocol. A straight table with a wedge cushion, traction, or the leg hanging free with the knee in flexion are all viable options. 

In comminuted fractures it is useful to drape the contralateral leg so that it is visible for comparison during rotational fracture reduction. 

Crucial part of pre-operative planning is to template the needed nail from the x-ray, so that you have the right implants in shelf when starting the operation. For example, with very small patients, ensure that the diameter of the medullary canal of tibia is sufficient for the available implants. 

For patients with suspected ACS already before operation, it is necessary to open the compartments of the lower leg prior to the fracture fixation. (see the two-incision fasciotomy of the lower leg here) 

Fracture reductionVerify that you can take adequate anterior-posterior (AP) and lateral views with the C-arm. 

Reduction is performed either in a closed or open manner. Percutaneous reduction with a bone reduction clamp is often sufficient for maintaining the reduction while reaming. Sometimes, however, a small incision is necessary to aid the reduction. In proximal shaft fractures, a small unicortical plate can aid in resisting fracture re-dislocations when reaming and/or introducing the nail. 

A temporary external fixation can aid the reduction, especially if no assistant is available to hold the reduction during intramedullary reaming. 

After reduction the correct rotation, length and alignment of the fracture must be carefully evaluated. 

Skin incisionThe correct medio-lateral position of the incision can be determined using the C-arm. Place a guide pin longitudinally on the skin, on top of the intramedullary canal of tibia, and mark the correct incision site on the skin.

Palpate patella and the patellar tendon.

The incision is placed vertically on the patellar tendon.

The incision starts proximally from the distal pole of the patella and runs distally in line with the patellar tendon until the level of the tibial plateau. 

Exposing the nail entry site and placing the guide pin There are various ways of handling the patellar tendon depending on the planned nail entry point. Here we go through the patellar tendon. 

After exposing the patellar tendon identify the medial and lateral borders of the tendon and open from the middle of the tendon length wise following the tendon fibers until we see the infrapatellar fat tissue (Hoffa’s fat pad). 

Use a self-retaining retractor to expose the proximal tibial plateau.

Manually palpate the anterior corner of the tibial plateau. 

In case of abundant interposing Hoffa’s fat pad, remove some of it. However, be cautious not to remove the fat too generously, as this could result in accidentally ending up inside the knee joint and possibly severing the intermeniscal ligament (transverse ligament of the knee). 



For inserting the guide pin use the C-arm to ensure the correct position. Place the tip of the pin just inferior to the anterior corner of the tibial plateau (lateral view), centered on the intramedullary canal (AP-view). Introduce the pin approximately 10 cm parallel to the tibial shaft axis, in order to avoid piercing the posterior cortex. 

Open the cortex Open the cortex of proximal tibia using a cannulated awl or a cannulated drill (5 cm is deep enough). Make sure to re-verify correct entry point - as described above for guide pin insertion – when opening the cortex. 

Inserting the guide wire Introduce a ball-tip guide wire into the intramedullary canal and pass it across the fracture. Bending the tip makes it easier to direct the guide wire past the fracture and into the desired location in the distal tibia. 

Align the guide wire to the center of the ankle joint, approximately 1 cm proximal to the joint line. Check the location using the C-arm with AP- and lateral views. 

Measure the length of the nail using the measuring instrument from the nailing kit.

Reaming Start reaming with the narrowest reamer. 

Check with the image intensifier that the reamer has passed all the way to the distal ball of the guide wire. 

Hold the guide wire in place while pulling the reamer from the tibia. If the guide wire comes out, push it back and re-check to ensure correct location with the C-arm. 

Ream with 0.5 mm increments, until reaching 1-2 mm past the desired nail diameter. The ‘cortical sound’ of the reamer at the isthmic area of the shaft is the first sign of encountering the maximal reaming diameter. 

In comminuted or segmental fractures, be careful when reaming the fracture zone. Sometimes the reaming can be stopped and advance the reamer gently through the fracture area and continue reaming below the fracture area again. 
In segmental fractures, a separate butterfly fragment should be kept in place with a reduction clamp to prevent rotation of the fragment during reaming. And to aid the fragment correctly apposing to the fracture in the end. 

Nail insertion Introduce the nail of chosen diameter and length over the guide wire and hammer it carefully across the fracture towards the ankle joint, stopping just before reaching it. Make sure the nail is inside the medullary canal form the proximal end. 

Ensure correct depth of the nail using the C-arm. 

Proximal and distal locking of the nail Check the correct rotation of the reduction before locking the nail. 

Attach the aiming device. Firstly, place the proximal locking screws in a medial-to-lateral direction. 

Make a small skin incision and bluntly advance to the bone surface of the tibia. Blunt dissection is recommended in order to avoid damage to the great saphenous vein, its branches or even to saphenous nerve. 

Drill using a specific drill sleeve and use nail-specific locking screws. 

In proximal shaft fractures, at least three proximal locking screws are recommended. In mid-shaft and distal shaft fractures two proximal locking screws are sufficient. Generally, one of the medio-lateral screws is placed in the dynamic hole and the other in the static hole. 

After proximal locking, if malrotation is observed, it is still possible to adjust the rotation of the fracture. 

Optionally, distal locking can be performed first to enable counter-hammering of the nail if diastasis between the fragments is seen. 

Distal screws are placed using free-hand technique. The C-arm is used to obtain images where the distal locking holes are perfectly round. 

In mid-shaft fractures two distal screws placed in a medio-lateral direction are enough. In distal fractures, one or two diagonal and/or AP screws are used in addition. The incision is made at the correct location under C-arm guidance. 

In medial skin incisions, blunt dissection is recommended. 

After drilling, the nail-specific screws are placed. 

When using diagonal and AP-screws, it is advisable to perform open exposure of the drilling location, ensuring good visibility of the bone cortex, to avoid damaging neurovascular structures. 

It is advisable to put an end cap on the proximal end of the nail to enable easier nail removal if deemed necessary later. To facilitate end cap application, keep the knee fully flexed. 

Wound closure Check for adequate hemostasis before closure. 
Close the patellar tendon with interrupted sutures (Vicryl no 1)

Close the paratenon and subcutaneous layers separately, using running absorbable sutures.

Close the skin with interrupted sutures or skin staples.

The incisions made inserting the locking screws can be closed in one layer by just closing the skin.

Aftercare General guidelines:Full weight bearing is usually allowed immediately in transverse fractures, and toe-touch weight bearing in oblique and segmental fractures. Careful monitoring for ACS symptoms should continue after the operation and prompt fasciotomies performed in patients with suspected ACS.

General

Medical experts: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon), Jenni Liikanen (orthopedic resident) 

Name of the Procedure: Intramedullary nailing of the tibia 

Problem

Tibial shaft fracture 



Goal of the Operation

Treatment of tibial shaft fracture by reduction and internal fixation. A metal nail is inserted in the intramedullary canal of the tibia to keep the bone fragments in an appropriate position during the healing process. 

Diagnosis

Fracture of shaft of tibia (ICD-10: S82.2) 

Fracture of lower end of tibia (ICD-10: S82.3) 

Short Pathophysiological Description

Tibial shaft fractures are usually caused by high-energy trauma (traffic accidents, falls from height, sports injuries) but are also seen in low energy torsional injuries, especially in elderly patients. 

Key Anatomical Structures 

Patella 

Patellar tendon 

Tibia 

Crest of tibia 

Femur 

Transverse ligament of the knee (i.e. intermeniscal ligament) 

Muscle compartments of the lower leg (see: link to the lower limb fasciotomy here) 

Anterior 

Lateral 

Superficial posterior 

Deep posterior 

Great saphenous vein 

Saphenous nerve 

Pitfalls

Inadequate fracture reduction

A common reason for problems in fracture healing is insufficient reduction of fractures. Causing either delayed union (fracture diastasis) or malunion due to malrotation or malalignment. Additionally, inadequate reduction makes it difficult to pass the guide wire across the fracture site during surgery. 

Incorrect incision site 

A misplaced incision can create difficulties in correct placement of the guide wire into the midline of the tibial shaft and difficulties while reaming. Pay attention to the landmarks and use the image intensifier and a guidewire to determine the optimal position for the incision. 

An incision made too medial or lateral may lead to difficulties in correct placement of the guide wire and difficulties while reaming.

Incorrect nail entry site

Due to incorrect incision site, there is a risk of incorrect nail entry. Incorrect placement of the guide wire in distal tibia causes fracture mal alignment, especially in distal shaft fractures. There is risk of positioning the nail too medially or laterally, or mal position it even in the anterior-posterior direction. In all these situations there is a risk of mal-reduction of the fracture causing e.g., varus or valgus type malalignment. 

The guide wire should be at the center with respect to the ankle joint, approximately 1cm proximal from the joint line. Make sure to check the location using image intensifier with AP- and lateral views.

Failure to identify acute compartment syndrome

In cases of associated compartment syndrome, the muscle compartments need to be promptly released with a fasciotomy to avoid permanent muscle damage and functional impairment. See the two-incision fasciotomy of the lower leg here. 

Careless tissue handling

Too aggressive reaming with a too wide reamer can cause thermal damage to the bone.

If the guide wire is pulled accidentally upwards during reaming and put back negligently, the guide wire can end up outside of the bone through the fracture gap. Continuation of reaming could result in damage to surrounding soft tissues. You can support the guide wire when pulling back the reamer.

Branches of the greater saphenous vein can be damaged when placing the proximal locking screws. Blunt dissection to expose the screw entry points is recommended. In addition, careless drilling of anterior and oblique distal locking screw could also cause injury to neurovascular structures. 

Inadequate planning

Failure to identify the narrow intramedullary canal in the tibial shaft before surgery will put the patient at risk for thermal damage due to excessive reaming and/or iatrogenic tibial fractures and bone loss.

Complications

Acute Compartment Syndrome (ACS)

In cases of trauma, the patient may develop acute compartment syndrome. Failure to perform fasciotomy for patients with acute compartment syndrome results in a high risk for permanent functional impairment. 

Careful monitoring for ACS symptoms should continue after the operation and prompt fasciotomies performed in patients with suspected ACS. 

Delayed fracture union or malalignment 

Inadequate reduction of the fracture leads to elevated risk of delayed fracture union or malalignment.


Inability to identify the correct place for nail entry elevates the risk for fracture malalignment (especially proximal fractures). 

Incorrect placement of the guide wire in the distal tibia causes fracture malalignment, especially in distal shaft fractures. Align the guide wire to the center of the ankle joint approximately 1 cm proximal to the joint line. Make sure to check the location using image intensifier with AP- and lateral views. 

Thermal Damage

Excessive reaming can lead to osteonecrosis and can compromise the bone-implant interface in fracture fixation. 

Neurovascular injury

Branches of the greater saphenous vein can be damaged when placing the proximal locking screws. Blunt dissection to expose the screw entry points is recommended. 

In addition, careless drilling of anterior and oblique distal locking screw could also cause injury to neurovascular structures. 



Postoperative hematoma formation 

Inability to perform a proper hemostasis increases the risk for postoperative hematoma formation and wound leakage.

title: Hip hemiarthroplasty - posterior approach
secondary_keywords: hemiarthroplasty posterior approach technique; femoral neck fracture hemiarthroplasty steps; posterior hip hemiarthroplasty procedure; bipolar hemiarthroplasty operative technique; hip fracture arthroplasty approach
all_keywords: hip hemiarthroplasty posterior approach; hemiarthroplasty posterior approach technique; femoral neck fracture hemiarthroplasty steps; posterior hip hemiarthroplasty procedure; bipolar hemiarthroplasty operative technique; hip fracture arthroplasty approach
