source_knowledge:
Video Surgeon: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon)

StepsPreparation and fracture reduction The patient is supine on the operating (fracture) table.
Either general or spinal anesthesia can be used.
The contralateral leg is placed on a leg support and abducted as far as possible to keep it out of the way when taking fluoroscopic images with the C-arm.

The upper body should be bent towards the contralateral side from the fracture to facilitate proper insertion of the nail. The ipsilateral arm should be placed on the trunk for example in a sling so it does not block nail insertion.
Before starting the reduction make sure that appropriate AP and lateral images can be obtained with the C-arm. Change the position of the contralateral leg if necessary. 
Apply axial traction.
After restoring limb length, internally rotate the leg (in hip fractures, the injured leg is usually externally rotated as a consequence of the fracture).
It is essential that the leg is brought into slight adduction to allow successful insertion of the nail from the tip of the greater trochanter.
If adequate reduction cannot be achieved using closed reduction, prepare for open reduction. Reduction should be as anatomical as possible.

Planning the incision site Palpate the landmarks: greater trochanter.

Determine the site of the incision using, for example, the help of a guide wire placed on the skin surface. Since the nail is inserted from the proximal direction, the incision will always be proximal to the greater trochanter.

Skin incision and opening of the fascia Make an incision proximal to the tip of the greater trochanter.

The incision should follow the longitudinal femoral axis. 
Use a blade or diathermy to advance through the subcutaneous fat. Use an Adson retractor to improve visibility. There are no critical anatomical structures just below the skin incision. 

Advance distally towards the tip of the greater trochanter and open the fascia using a blade or diathermy.
Palpate the trochanteric tip. 

Nail insertion site Insert the nail from the trochanteric tip and at the junction of the anterior and middle thirds of the trochanter. 

Introduce the guide wire to the tip of the trochanter. Confirm the location of the wire from an AP image. Correct the position of the wire if necessary. When the position appears good on the AP view, insert it into the bone approximately 1cm deep and then check the correct location from the lateral view. 

For the lateral projection, the C-arm should be tilted about 15 degrees to be in alignment with the femoral neck anteversion to obtain a so called true lateral view. This facilitates evaluation of the position and direction of the guide wire.

Guide wire insertion Drill the guide wire further into the femur checking by fluoroscopy that it does not end up outside the bone via the fracture site.

Reaming of the proximal femur Ream the proximal femur using the appropriate reamer and protective reamer sleeve. 

Avoid using strong compressive force when reaming as this may spread the fracture fragments apart. This may happen especially if the fracture continues to the tip of the trochanter, which is quite common.
Take fluoroscopic images when reaming to make sure the reamer is advancing correctly. 

The reamer automatically stops at the correct depth if the protective reamer sleeve tip is properly placed against the tip of the greater trochanter. 

Nail insertion Choose a nail with the suitable femoral neck angle, based on preoperative measurement.
Insert the nail into the femur initially by hand over the guide wire and, if necessary, tap the nail deeper with the mallet. 

Determining the correct nail position The correct nail rotation and depth are based on correct positioning of the femoral neck lag screw. To facilitate determining the correct location for the lag screw you can use a targeting tool called, the ‘one shot device’, which is connected to the guide sleeve of the lag screw. Set the nail to the correct depth (AP view) and rotation using this device (lateral view). 

The femoral neck lag screw should be at the junction of the middle and the lower thirds of the femoral neck in the AP view and in the middle of the femoral neck in the true lateral view.

Femoral neck guide wire Make a small skin incision where the guide sleeve of the femoral neck lag screw touches the skin. 

Open the fascia with a blade or scissors. 

Put the guide sleeve against the lateral cortex of the femur. 

Using a drill, open the lateral cortex, this will help reduce the risk of guide wire deflection. 

Change the trocar of the guide sleeve for the guide wire. 

Drill the guide wire close to the subchondral bone aiming to minimize tip-apex distance and confirm the correct location of the guide wire from AP and lateral views.

Measure the length of the lag screw using the appropriate measuring device. Note: The measure automatically subtracts 10mm to directly give the appropriate lag screw length. However, before choosing the lag screw, evaluate the distance of the guide wire to the joint surface. Keep in mind that the goal is to have a tip-apex distance (TAD) of under 25 mm for the lag screw at the end of the procedure. The TAD is the sum of the distances between the tip of the femoral lag screw and the midpoint of the articular surface as measured from the AP and lateral projections.

Femoral neck lag screw placement Adjust the step drill in accordance with the planned lag screw length and drill the femoral neck along the guide wire. 

If the guide wire comes off while drilling, insert it again and confirm the correct placement by fluoroscopy.
Screw the femoral lag screw into position under fluoroscopy and leave the insertion tool handle either parallel with or perpendicular to the nail. 

In basal femoral neck fractures, it is advisable to use a separate, temporarily placed anti-rotation pin to prevent femoral head rotation when placing the femoral lag screw. 

Inserting the set screw into the nail Put the set screw (which prevents rotation of the femoral neck lag screw relative to the nail) using the set screw guide. 

First tighten the screw fully and make sure there is no rotational movement on the lag screw handle. Then unscrew the set screw about quarter (¼) of a turn so that there is slight movement allowing the femoral neck screw to slide but preventing it from rotating (unscrewing). 
If you think the nail may need to be removed in the future, you can place an end cap on the upper end of the nail so as to prevent bone ingrowth.

Placing the distal locking screwBefore placing the distal locking screw make sure that the nail target device is still tightly in place. Make a small incision at the site where the distal locking screw guide touches the skin. Open the fascia with a blade or scissors. 

Using the drill guide, drill a hole through the femur. Take care not to drill too medially (past the medial cortex) to avoid injury to the deep femoral artery or vein.

Measure the appropriate screw length with the help of the scale on the drill and fluoroscopy. 

Place the screw into position and check the position by fluoroscopy. 

Final images and wound closure Release the traction and take the final images, AP and true lateral.

Check hemostasis and cauterize bleeds if necessary.
Close the fascia and the subcutaneous layer of the proximal incision with an absorbable braided suture (e.g. Vicryl 0 or 1). The incisions used for placement of the femoral neck lag screw and the distal locking screw can be closed with surgical staples or simple interrupted skin sutures.

Aftercare General guidelines: Full weight-bearing is usually allowed immediately, particularly in elderly patients.

General

Medical experts: Lasse Rämö (orthopedic surgeon), Rami Madanat (orthopedic surgeon), Jenni Liikanen (orthopedic resident)

Name of Procedure: Cephalomedullary nailing of an intertrochanteric fracture of the proximal femur using the trochanteric nail. 

Goal of Operation

Reduction and stable fixation of an intertrochanteric fracture of the proximal femur with immediate restoration of full weight-bearing.

Problem

Intertrochanteric fracture of the proximal femur

Diagnosis

Pertrochanteric fracture (ICD-10: S72.1) 

Short Pathophysiological Description

Intertrochanteric femoral fractures are usually caused by a same-level fall and are common in elderly people with osteoporosis. 

Key anatomical structures

Femoral neck 

Femoral shaft 

Greater trochanter 

Deep femoral artery and vein (drilling for insertion of the distal locking screw)

Pitfalls

Incorrect incision site 

The incorrect incision site will make it difficult to achieve correct placement of the guide wire into the trochanteric tip and parallel with the femoral diaphysis. You can use fluoroscopy and a guide wire placed on top of the skin to determine the correct site of incision. 

Inadequate knowledge of anatomy and inadequate planning 

The anatomy of the femoral neck and the neck-shaft angle vary between patients. The varus/valgus angle and the ante/retroversion of the femoral neck are also unique to the individual. This is why planning of the operation and choosing the correct implant is important. Preoperative planning is done using the hip AP X-ray of the intact contralateral side. 

Inadequate understanding of biomechanics 

The aim is to achieve a biomechanically stable fixation. For stability, it is vital to achieve good (anatomic) reduction and correct positioning of the nail and the femoral lag screw. Incorrect placement of the nail or the femoral lag screw carries the risk of fracture dislocation and cut-out of the screw through the femoral head articular surface. The location of the nail and the femoral lag screw is determined by the guide wire placement, which must be done with care. 

The sum of the distances between the tip of the femoral lag screw and the midpoint of the articular surface as measured from the AP and lateral projections must be <25 mm (TAD= tip-apex distance) in order to minimize the risk of the lag screw cutting out and fixation failure. 

Intraoperative orientation challenges 

Pay special attention to the lateral projection since it may be deceiving when taking intraoperative fluoroscopic images. In uncertain cases, an instrument can be placed in the image area to confirm correct orientation (anterior vs. posterior), especially when adjusting the rotation of the nail while positioning the femoral neck guide wire. 

Careless tissue handling 

The deep femoral artery can be injured during guide wire placement or drilling the distal locking screw. Fluoroscopy will help you avoid placing the guide wire too medially and thus endangering the medial structures. Drilling through the medial femoral cortex in placement of the distal locking screw must be done carefully not to drill with too much force and injuring the deep femoral artery.

Complications 

Deep femoral artery injury 

Careless drilling of the femur after penetrating the medial cortex causes a risk of blood vessel injury (deep femoral artery). 

Loosening of the implant and loss of fracture reduction

Inadequate reduction causes an increased risk of implant loosening and fixation failure. If a closed reduction does not result in an anatomically satisfactory result, an open reduction should be performed. Malreduction may also result from incorrect positioning of the nail guide wire or the femoral lag screw guide wire, eventually resulting in incorrect positioning of the implant. 

Incorrect nail positioning 

If the guide wire is inserted into the trochanter too laterally, the lateral wall of the trochanter may be injured during reaming and this will compromise fracture stability.

Incorrect femoral lag screw positioning 

Incorrect positioning of the femoral lag screw guide wire is caused by the incorrect depth and/or rotation of the nail. This results in incorrect positioning of the femoral lag screw (>25 mm distance between the screw tip and the femoral head apex). An incorrectly placed femoral lag screw makes the implant prone to loosening, loss of fracture reduction and cut-out of the screw tip through the articular surface of the femoral head. This can be avoided by ensuring the correct depth and rotation of the nail and by placing the femoral neck guide wire carefully into the correct position close to the subchondral bone of the articular surface of the femoral head. 

Postoperative hematoma and bleeding 

To avoid the risk of postoperative hematoma and bleeding, good intraoperative hemostatic control is vital. A check should be made before wound closure to ensure that there is no significant bleeding. 

Soft tissue irritation 

A femoral neck lag screw that is too long may cause lateral soft tissue irritation. To avoid this, the screw length must be determined correctly. Before choosing the lag screw evaluate the distance of the guide wire to the joint surface. If the guide wire is very close to the subchondral bone do not round up when choosing the length of the lag screw.

title: Full thickness skin graft
secondary_keywords: FTSG surgical steps; full thickness graft harvest procedure; skin graft inset technique; reconstructive full thickness skin graft; full thickness graft donor site closure
all_keywords: full thickness skin graft technique; FTSG surgical steps; full thickness graft harvest procedure; skin graft inset technique; reconstructive full thickness skin graft; full thickness graft donor site closure
source_knowledge_excerpt: VideoSurgeon: Hannu Kuokkanen (plastic surgeon) Steps   Planning the donor siteThe aim is to harvest a skin graft that, by its properties, matches the site of the skin defect as well as possible. In areas with skin creasing (ear, groin), the midline of the graft should be harvested in the middle of the flexure. This enables the best direct closure of the...
source_knowledge:
VideoSurgeon: Hannu Kuokkanen (plastic surgeon)
Steps 

Planning the donor siteThe aim is to harvest a skin graft that, by its properties, matches the site of the skin defect as well as possible. Although the graft could theoretically be taken from any location the following aspects should be kept in mind when choosing the donor site:

Primary closure: The donor site for the graft should be chosen with direct wound closure in mind. In areas with skin creasing (ear, groin), the midline of the graft should be harvested in the middle of the flexure. This enables the best direct closure of the donor site.

Graft size: The size of the graft is defined by the size of the defect.
Thickness of dermis: The thicker the dermis, the better it preserves the properties of the skin. However, too thick dermis risks revascularization.
Color match: For the face, the donor site should be above the clavicular level for an acceptable color match. The most common donor sites for the face are behind the ears and the supraclavicular fossa. For elsewhere in the body, a full thickness skin graft can be taken from the inguinal area, for example.
Hair follicles: Hair follicles are located in the dermis layer of skin. It is not recommended to transfer a skin graft with hair follicles to a site which normally would not have any hair but then again if the skin is transferred to the top of the head, it should contain hair follicles.

Measuring the defectThe aim is to have the graft size matching the skin defect size precisely. Too small graft might rupture when sutured in place whereas too large graft ends up wrinkling compromising revascularisation as the wound bed and the graft are not in direct contact with each other.
Make a template of the skin defect by using sterile paper (e.g. from a suture pack). The template should match the size of the skin defect precisely.

Preparations and local anaesthesiaThe donor site is prepped with surgical wash. Sterile adhesive drapes are used to designate the sterile operating field. When performing a full thickness skin graft on a face the entire face should be prepped and covered using three drapes. 

Use a small needle and inject the local anesthetic (most commonly lidocaine 0.5 - 1% with adrenaline) gently whilst advancing the needle through the already numbed area. Administer more anesthetic on withdrawal of the needle to avoid too much pressure within the tissue.
It’s recommended to use a syringe with a threaded hub (for example Luer Lock) so that the needle won’t come off the syringe on withdrawal. 
Wait for at least 5 minutes after administering the anesthetic and make sure that the whole surgical field has been fully anesthetized before proceeding. If necessary, use more anesthetic.

Graft detachment The goal is to end up with a right sized skin graft with just the epidermis and the superficial layer of the dermis because a graft with fatty tissue will not revascularize from the wound bed. 
When removing a potentially malignant skin lesion it is recommended to take off your sterile gloves and put on a new pair once you have completed the excision of the lesion. Furthermore, continue with a new set of surgical instruments to avoid the potential tumor cell spreading
Use the template to trace the graft donor size.

Start incising the epidermis and the dermis perpendicular to the stretched skin according to the traced plan.

The graft is taken with a thin superficial layer of the dermis, the deep layer of the dermis is left intact. When only the superficial layer of the dermis is removed with the graft, a thin layer of the deeper dermis will be seen on top of the subcutaneous fatty tissue. 

Hold the detached skin graft on your fingertip and carefully remove all fatty tissue with sharp scissors. If the fatty tissue is not removed the graft will not revascularize from the wound bed.

When the fatty tissue has been properly removed the graft should be translucent. 

After the graft has been detached it shrinks and may look too small for the transfer site. However, since the template has been used, the graft will be the right size.

The graft is put into saline to wait for the transplantation.

Closure of the donor siteThe donor site should be closed with primary closure. If there is tension you may need to remove some of the fatty tissue or undermine the wound edges. 
If necessary, excise subcutaneous fat from the donor site to facilitate primary closure of the wound.
If there is still tension not allowing for primary closure, undermine the skin edges in the deeper layer.

It’s recommended to leave some dermis at the wound edges. The dermis at the wound edges will help with revascularization.
Close the dermis with absorbable monofilament thread (for example Monocryl/Biosyn). Take firm inverted bites through the dermis without perforating the epidermis so that the knot will be buried deep (inverted suture). This helps avoid suture fistulas as the absorbable suture knot is not directly next to the epidermis.

Dermal sutures are mainly responsible for holding the tension of the skin and help bring the wound edges within reach of each other.

Once the wound edges are within reach of each other, close the epidermal layer using interrupted non-absorbable nylon stitches or a continuous suture.

Attaching the graftThe goal is to attach the graft with optimal conditions for revascularization. Revascularization will happen both from the wound bed and the dermis at the wound edges. The graft is anchored in place with both absorbable and nonabsorbable sutures. The absorbable sutures will help match the dermis at the graft and wound edges. In turn, the nonabsorbable sutures will be holding sutures used to tie the wound dressings in place.
The skin graft is placed on the recipient site and first attached with nonabsorbable nylon sutures. Place the first suture at a corner and continue with the opposite corner to get the graft precisely in place. 

Use, for example, 6 long threads at 60-degree intervals for the tie-over dressing leaving 10-15 cm at the ends of the sutures to tie around the dressings.

Place an adequate number of stitches with absorbable suture in between these long threads to ensure proper attachment of the graft. The graft edges should be in contact with the dermis at the wound edges for optimal revascularization.

Applying a compression dressingAim is to create soft pressure on the graft so it will be in contact with the wound bed promoting revascularization and minimizing hematoma formation. Hematoma would lift the graft from the wound bed risking revascularization.
First, cover the graft with an appropriately sized paraffin gauze / silicone dressing so that the fluffed gauze pads on top wont stick to the graft. Use the template to cut the paraffin gauze to match the graft. 

The paraffin gauze is covered by damp, fluffed gauze pads and then with a small stack of smooth gauze pads.

Tie the opposing long sutures over the gauze pads to create a small amount of pressure on the dressings. The assistant can temporarily press on the knot to retain tension during tying the knots using Mosquito forceps, for example.

If the skin graft is performed on a limb, it’s recommended to protect the gauze pads and the holding sutures with a spiral bandage.

AftercareGeneral guidelines:

The gauze pads should be kept in place for 5 days and then removed with care. The dried dressing should be treated with saline until it softens. Next, the nylon holding sutures are cut off and the gauze pads are removed along with the paraffin gauze. Use forceps to prevent the graft from lifting off the wound surface with the paraffin gauze. The absorbable sutures at the edges of the graft can remain in place. The graft is covered again by a paraffin gauze, dry gauze pads and a wound dressing. 

At 7-10 days postoperatively all sutures will be removed from both the graft transfer and the donor site. 

Depending on the location, the graft should be protected against rubbing or irritation for another 2-4 weeks.

General

Medical expert: Hannu Kuokkanen (plastic surgeon)

Name of procedure: Full thickness skin graft

Goal of operation

Repair of a skin defect with a full thickness skin graft

Problem

Skin defect where primary closure is not possible, or a local flap is not an option

Diagnosis

Other or unspecified skin changes (ICD-10: R23.8)



Short pathophysiological description

Depending on the anatomical site, the skin defect can be repaired by primary closure, a skin transposition flap, a full thickness skin graft or a split thickness skin graft. A full thickness skin graft is a useful method for the repair of minor skin defects, particularly in the facial area if primary closure is not possible. A full thickness skin graft yields a good esthetic outcome with minimal scarring. 

Key anatomical structures

Epidermis 

Dermis 

Subcutis 

Fascia 

Subcutaneous nerves in the surgical field, for example: 

Facial area: the temporal branch and the marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve

Upper limb: Antebrachial cutaneous nerve of the forearm, superficial radial branch

Lower limb: lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh, saphenous nerve, superficial fibular nerve, sural nerve.

Pitfalls

The graft is too thin 

If the skin graft is too thin it may rupture when attached. A few small holes are acceptable as these will allow drainage of any hematoma. A torn graft can be sutured. If this is not successful, a new graft is needed. 





The graft is too thick 

If the graft is too thick it may not revascularize. Once the graft has been detached, carefully remove all fatty tissue with sharp scissors. When the fatty tissue has been properly removed the graft should be translucent.



The graft has been taken from a wrong site 

This may result in a color mismatch which is especially significant in the facial area. A full thickness skin graft for the face is taken from supraclavicular skin, usually from behind the ear or from the clavicular fossa. Also, be mindful of hair follicles and do not transfer skin with prominent hair follicles on the patient’s face, for example. 



The graft is not the right size 

If the graft is too small there may be tension and it will rupture, whereas too large a graft will wrinkle, compromising revascularization as the wound bed and the graft surface are no in contact with each other. To establish the right size, a sterile template of the skin lesion excision site should be used. 

Inadequate hemostasis

Pay attention to adequate hemostasis. A hematoma might risk revascularization by lifting the graft from the wound bed. Also, wound hematoma may be susceptible to infection, slow the healing or cause wound dehiscence.

Complications 

Graft necrosis 

Graft necrosis, which means failure to revascularize, may result from the graft being too thick or a hematoma under the graft. The necrotic part should be removed but the viable part of the graft should be left in place. After this, a non-stick dressing will be applied, and it should be assessed later on whether a new graft is needed or if the graft is healing on its own. 

Hematoma

Wound hematoma may be susceptible to infection, slow the healing or cause wound dehiscence. 

If the wound continues to bleed at home or a large hematoma swelling develops, the patient should be advised to contact the surgical unit or the ER. Haemorrhaging can be persistent, particularly in patients on anticoagulants. 

Infection 

Both donor and recipient site wounds may become infected. Mild infection is treated locally and possibly with antibiotics. If there is a deep suppurative infection, the sutures should be removed, and the wound left open. The open wound should be covered with a moist cleansing antiseptic dressing and changed every 1-2 days. A new graft can be considered once the infection has cleared. 

Nerve injury  

Sensory loss around the wound may be due to skin nerve injury caused by stretching or severing a nerve. Loss of sensation in a minor area is usually harmless but severing a sensory nerve serving a larger area should be referred to a specialist (hand surgeon or plastic surgeon). 

If there is loss of function in the facial area (e.g., forehead muscle paresis and dropping of the eyebrow), a plastic surgeon should be consulted.

Scar hypertrophy 

Some patients may have a strong tendency to scar formation. This should be asked about beforehand. After the wound has healed, if there is itching, pain, erythema and swelling of the scar, the patient may have scar hypertrophy. Scar hypertrophy should be treated actively and proactively.

title: Finger joint (MCP, PIP, DIP) injections

source_knowledge:
VideoDoctor: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist)
Steps Patient positioning and preparations The patient is seated with the affected hand palm-down on their lap. A cushion is placed beneath the hand for support.

For example, a brown needle 26G (0.45mm x 13mm) or an orange needle 25G (0,50 x 16 mm) is suitable for finger joints.

For anti-inflammatory treatment and for alleviating symptoms of osteoarthritis methylprednisolone 40 mg/ml can be used; 0.2-0.3 ml for MCP and PIP-joints and 0.1-0.2 ml for DIP-joint. 

MCP joint The MCP joint space is located by palpating just distal to the knuckle. By lifting the joint space from the palmar side, the joint is felt more distinctly

If the joint space isn't clearly palpable due to swelling, the finger can be slightly pulled to make it more distinctive.

Alternatively, the MCP joint can be injected through the joint recess, in which case it is advisable to keep the finger straight. Flexing the finger tenses the recess, making it harder to target.

The recess rises to the proximal side of the joint, where swelling is usually also visible.
The extensor tendon running in the midline should be avoided, so the injection is performed from the ulnar or radial side.

The injection area is cleaned. 

The level of the joint space can be palpated from the opposite side of the tendon, without contaminating the cleaned injection site. A slight pull is maintained on the flexed finger to open the joint space.

The needle is aimed underneath the extensor tendon. Usually, a depth of 7 to 8 mm is enough to reach the joint space. 

The medicinal agent appropriate to the situation is injected. The capsule tightening can be felt as the joint fills. An appropriate volume is usually about 0.2 - 0.3 ml.

Landmarks and injection site - PIP and DIP joints Both PIP and DIP joint injections are usually performed into the joint recess, since the joint space is often not that distinctive.
The finger should be extended to relax the recess. If the finger is flexed, the recess becomes tense, making it harder to target.

When targeting the recess and not the actual joint space, the injection is performed just proximal to where the joint space level is located.

To avoid the extensor tendon, the injection is again performed from radial or ulnar side.

The injection area is cleaned. 

The area around the affected joint is squeezed to move synovial fluid into the recess, causing the joint capsule to bulge and making it easier to access. 

The needle is inserted between the surface of the bone and the extensor tendon in a horizontal plane.

The medicinal agent appropriate to the situation is injected. There is typically very low resistance in the joint capsule. Usually, 0.1 - 0.2 ml can be injected into the DIP joint and 0.2 - 0.3 into the PIP joint.

Aftercare General guidelines: 

The injection site is covered with an adhesive bandage and keep it dry and clean for 24 hours to minimize the risk of infection. 
For a better therapeutic effect, stressing of the injected joint for 24-48 hours and avoid heavy strain for one week.

General

Medical expert: Joonas Rautavaara (rheumatologist)

Procedure: MCP-, PIP- and DIP - joint injections 



Goal

Safe access to the finger joints for synovial fluid sampling and/or local treatment.

Diagnosis

Rheumatoid arthritis, unspecified (ICD-10: M06.9) 

Arthropathic psoriasis (ICD-10: M07.3*L40.5)

Polyosteoarthritis, unspecified (ICD-10: M15.9)

Idiopathic gout (ICD-10: M10.0)

Short pathophysiological description

MCP, PIP, and DIP joint injections are commonly performed for inflammatory arthropathies such as rheumatoid arthritis, where synovial inflammation causes synovial hypertrophy and an increased production of synovial fluid in the joint, leading to joint swelling, pain and limited mobility. Osteoarthritis affecting these joints, particularly common in the DIP joints, may also warrant injection therapy. Other causes include crystal arthropathies like gout or pseudogout, septic arthritis requiring diagnostic aspiration, and traumatic joint effusions following injury.

Key anatomical structures 

Metacarpal bones 

MCP, PIP and DIP joints

Joint capsules and joint recesses 

Extensor tendons of the fingers 

Pitfalls 

Wrong injection site

When injecting on the MCP joints, the extensor tendon should be avoided as injecting through these is extremely painful. Also, while injecting PIP and DIP joints, if the injection is given too far on the dorsal side, there is a risk of hitting the extensor tendon. 

Injecting too frequently 

In case of acute inflammation involving a small joint, like the radiocarpal joint, intra-articular injections can be given at 1-month intervals for the first 3 months. Afterwards, if needed, 6 weeks apart. 

There is no strong evidence of adverse effects of glucocorticoids on the joint, although frequent injections may cause unwanted systemic effects.

Complications

Infection

Intra-articular infection after a joint injection is very rare, the incidence being 1:2000-1:100 000, most commonly 1:10 000. Nevertheless, aseptic technique while doing the procedure should be maintained. 

Joint pain

Joint pain for 24 hours post-injection is an adverse effect occurring in up to 10% of cases, particularly with glucocorticoid injections. It may result from a local tissue injury caused by the needle alone but is usually due to an irritation reaction to the crystalline glucocorticoid or hyaluronate. The joint may be painful, perhaps even feel warm, for about 24 hours, but these symptoms ease spontaneously. The symptoms can be alleviated by analgesics or cold treatment. 

Hemarthrosis 

Intra-articular hemorrhage is a very rare complication of a joint injection. Antithrombotic treatment or anticoagulant medication is not a contraindication for intra-articular injection as the risk is very small, about 0-2%, even in patients using these medicines. In patients on anticoagulants, however, the smallest possible needle is recommended for joint injection. 

Local skin atrophy or hypopigmentation 

Local skin atrophy occurs in about 1% of the patients after intra-articular injection of glucocorticoids. Typically, atrophy at the injection area develops at 1-4 months post-injection. In addition to atrophy, there may be hypopigmentation around the injection area. Atrophy and hypopigmentation are caused by subcutaneous glucocorticoid accumulation, potentially due to extracapsular injection or extracapsular seepage of the glucocorticoid via the puncture canal. 

Pericapsular calcium deposit 

Pericapsular calcification, or calcium deposits around the joint capsule of the target joint is very common, occurring after intra-articular injections in up to 10% of cases. Pericapsular calcium deposits are usually asymptomatic and harmless. In most cases, calcium is spontaneously absorbed from the synovial capsule but in some patients X-ray images may show even permanent calcifications, particularly in small joints such as the PIP and the DIP joints of the fingers.

title: Femoral artery exposure
secondary_keywords: common femoral artery surgical approach; femoral vessel exposure trauma; groin vascular exposure steps; femoral artery operative anatomy; femoral artery cutdown procedure
all_keywords: femoral artery exposure technique; common femoral artery surgical approach; femoral vessel exposure trauma; groin vascular exposure steps; femoral artery operative anatomy; femoral artery cutdown procedure
source_knowledge_excerpt: VideoSurgeon: Ville Vänni (Trauma surgeon) The goal of this procedure is to achieve safe surgical exposure and vascular control of the proximal femoral arteries. The same principles of the approach apply to trauma requiring vascular control, limb ischemia needing endovascular treatment, and arterial disease requiring surgical or endovascular intervention. StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparationThe patient is positioned supine with the groin exposed  Anesthesia choice depends on the procedure, being typically general or spinal anesthesia, or local anesthesia for purely...
source_knowledge:
VideoSurgeon: Ville Vänni (Trauma surgeon)
The goal of this procedure is to achieve safe surgical exposure and vascular control of the proximal femoral arteries. The same principles of the approach apply to trauma requiring vascular control, limb ischemia needing endovascular treatment, and arterial disease requiring surgical or endovascular intervention.
StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparationThe patient is positioned supine with the groin exposed

Anesthesia choice depends on the procedure, being typically general or spinal anesthesia, or local anesthesia for purely endovascular procedures. 

Sterile preparation and draping depend on the indication. For vascular injuries, the lower abdomen is included in the sterile field to allow proximal extension for external iliac artery access if needed.

Approach goal and strategyThe goal is to access and gain control of the proximal femoral arteries: the common femoral artery, superficial femoral artery, and deep femoral artery.

Using the longitudinal femoral approach, this is accomplished by incising through skin and subcutaneous tissue, dividing the fascia layer, identifying the common femoral artery, and exposing its bifurcation into the superficial and deep femoral arteries.
Important structures to avoid injuring include the femoral nerve branches laterally to the artery and the femoral vein medially.

Landmarks and incision siteThe external iliac artery becomes the common femoral artery at the inguinal ligament level,

then bifurcates a few centimeters distally into the superficial femoral artery (SFA) (primary vessel to the lower extremity) and deep femoral artery (profunda femoris), (thigh collateral circulation).

The inguinal ligament extends from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the pubic tubercle. 

The palpation finding of the ligament on the skin (or the inguinal crease) is typically felt slightly more distal to the actual ligament. 

The skin incision is positioned at the inguinal ligament level, approximately midway between the ASIS and pubic tubercle. The artery lies just medial to the midpoint of the inguinal ligament. If the femoral pulse is palpable, this can guide precise medial-lateral positioning.
Since the palpable inguinal crease lies slightly distal to the actual ligament, the incision should start just proximal to the palpable landmark.

Incision length should be sufficient to achieve proximal vascular control at the common femoral artery level and distal control at the superficial and deep femoral artery.

The typical ptfall is starting too distally, which exposes only the superficial femoral artery rather than the common femoral artery and bifurcation site.

Skin incision and advancing to fasciaThe incision is made with a scalpel according to the planned line to the subcutaneous fat level.

The subcutaneous layer can be divided with a scalpel down to the fascia lata level. In live surgery, electrocautery is preferred for hemostasis.

Encountering the great saphenous vein trunk during dissection indicates the approach has gone too medial and should be redirected laterally.

Any encountered lymphatic vessels should be ligated.

The fascial surface becomes visible after dividing the fat layer.

A blunt Adson retractor is used to expose the fascia.

Advancing to artery surfaceThe aim is to expose the anterior surface of the femoral artery to the inguinal ligament level.

The fascia is incised with scissors using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection. 

If muscle fibers become visible beneath the fascia, this indicates the approach is too lateral and dissection should be redirected medially when advancing deeper.

if the femoral artery is pulsatile or its calcified plaques can be palpated, these would guide the advancement.

The retractor is repositioned to redirect the dissection medially.

As dissection progresses deeper, the arterial wall starts to become visible under the thin covering of the femoral sheath. 

The artery is exposed distally.

The vessel exposed can be identified as the artery, even without pulsation, by the characteristic vasa vasorum on its surface.

The femoral veins darker appearance is faintly visible medial to the artery. Lateral to the artery, the femoral nerve appears as multiple thin branches at this level.

With the anterior surface of the femoral artery exposed, basic puncture procedures could now be performed. For complete vascular control, however, the deep femoral artery bifurcation must be identified to establish anatomical landmarks and gain control of all three vessels: the superficial femoral, deep femoral, and common femoral arteries.

To define the proximal extent of exposure, the inguinal ligament can be palpated cranially within the wound. 

This is the proximal limit of femoral vessel exposure without dividing the inguinal ligament. If greater proximal control is needed, the inguinal ligament could be divided, though this requires identification and control of the inferior epigastric vessels and subsequent ligament repair during closure.

Exposing and identifying the femoral arteriesThe goal is to carefully dissect the vessel free from surrounding tissue to enable vascular control and to expose the deep femoral artery origin for vessel identification.

The vessel wall should be handled gently and avoided direct manipulation with instruments. Gentle technique is essential to avoid intimal damage, dissection, or dislodging atherosclerotic plaque.

During medial side dissection, care must be taken to protect the femoral vein.

With the vessel cleared of surrounding tissue, the larger caliber of the common femoral artery is visible transitioning to the smaller caliber superficial femoral artery marking the bifurcation point and the deep femoral artery origin.

Controlling the femoral arteriesThe goal is to gain vascular control proximally at the common femoral artery and distally at the superficial and deep femoral artery level.

A right-angle clamp is used to pass the vessel loop circumferentially around the artery. The instrument passage under the vessel should be done from medial to lateral to avoid injuring the femoral vein wall.

The loop is carefully passed under the artery. 

The first vessel to control here, is the deep femoral artery, so the loop is passed back and up from the other side of the branch.

Additional exposure of the deep femoral artery can be performed based on the need. To only achieve vascular control of this artery using vessel loops or clamps, this level of exposure is sufficient.

However, care should be taken to ensure the vessel loop encircles the main trunk and not merely a side branch. The deep femoral artery gives off branches quite proximally.

Distally, the circumflex veins crossing the deep femoral artery are visible. Care must be taken with these as they can cause considerable bleeding if injured.

The superficial femoral artery is looped next.

And then the common femoral artery. 

The vessel loop is passed twice around the artery, especially on the proximal side, to ensure occlusion in the event of clamp failure or displacement.

The femoral vessels are now controlled with the vessel loops and vascular clamps can now be placed if needed. The patient should be heparinized before vessel occlusion unless active bleeding necessitates immediate vascular control.

Clamp time of the vessels should be minimized. The tolerance to ischemia varies with the clinical scenario and how well the limb is adapted to chronic ischemia.Generally clamp times up to two hours are considered safe, but the longer the clamp is on, the more it increases metabolic effects during reperfusion.

With vascular control achieved proximally and distally, the vessels are now ready for the intended vascular procedure, whether repair, reconstruction or endovascular treatment.

Clamp removal and closure

Upon completion of the vascular procedure, if vessel suturing was performed, the repair site is vented by allowing blood flow from all directions through the suture line before final tightening of the sutures.

Following this, the vascular clamps are released. Typically, the deep femoral artery clamp is released first, the common femoral artery is then released, creating a preferential pathway for any residual embolic material. 

The superficial femoral artery clamp is released last. The anesthesia team is informed when clamps are released due to hemodynamic and reperfusion related metabolic changes.

Drain and hemostatic agent use depends on clinical circumstances. Groin wounds have increased infection risk, especially in peripheral arterial disease patients, which must be weighed against bleeding control needs.

The fascia layer is closed using absorbable suture material with running technique. Care must be taken when placing sutures to ensure accurate bite placement and avoid inadvertent inclusion of femoral nerve branches.

The subcurtaneous layer is closed with absorbable continuous sutures.If the great saphenous vein was encountered during dissection, it should not be included in the suture bites during subcutaneous closure. 

The skin layer can be closed with interrupted or continuous sutures, or by using surgical staples.

Surgical staples, while quick to apply, may pose challenges in cases where there is risk of postoperative bleeding, as they would need to be removed rapidly to allow immediate access to the surgical site for intervention.

AftercareGeneral guidelines: 

The aftercare protocols may vary between units and depends on type of procedure performed.

Anticoagulation protocols are planned in consultation with vascular surgery. Embolectomy patients typically receive systemic anticoagulation, while arterial repairs may receive antiplatelet therapy.

General

Medical experts: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon)

Name of procedure: Femoral artery exposure

Goal of Operation

To expose and gain vascular control of common femoral, superficial femoral and deep femoral arteries for treatment.

Problem

Hemorrhage or limb ischemia due to vascular injury or occlusive disease.

Diagnosis 

Trauma/injury

Unspecified injury of femoral artery S75.01

Ischemia

Embolism and thrombosis of arteries of the lower extremities I74.3

Unspecified atherosclerosis of native arteries of extremities I70.20

Short Pathophysiological Description

Femoral artery injuries occur through direct trauma (iatrogenic puncture, penetrating injury, blunt force) or iatrogenic access complications that damage the vessel wall. Arterial puncture too proximally above the femoral head prevents effective compression against bone, while through-and-through punctures might create posterior wall bleeding that is inaccessible for direct compression. The common femoral artery's short length (approximately 5 cm) and its position as the sole blood supply continuation to the lower extremity means that uncontrolled hemorrhage or thrombosis threatens limb viability.

Key anatomical structures:

Common femoral artery

Superficial femoral artery (or Femoral artery)

Deep femoral artery

Femoral circumflex arteries

Femoral vein

Deep femoral vein

Femoral circumflex veins

Great saphenous vein

Femoral nerve and its branches

Inguinal ligament

Anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS)

Pubic tubercle

Pitfalls 

Too distal incision site

Using the inguinal crease instead of the true inguinal ligament site as a landmark results in too-distal incision placement, exposing only the superficial femoral artery and missing the bifurcation level.

Incorrect medial-lateral incision placement

The incision should be centered midway between the ASIS and pubic tubercle. Too medial placement encounters the great saphenous vein (risking femoral vein injury), while too lateral placement encounters the sartorius muscle (risking femoral nerve injury). Encountering these structures indicates the need to adjust the dissection plane. The direction can be adjusted based on the palpable femoral pulse or plaques if present.

Careless tissue handling

Inadequate tissue handling in the subcutaneous layer can disrupt lymphatic vessels, leading to persistent lymphatic leakage, seroma formation, and delayed wound healing if these vessels are not clipped during dissection. The superficial veins, including branches of the great saphenous vein system, are also at risk during subcutaneous dissection and should be ligated or preserved.

Rough handling of the femoral vessels during dissection and looping can cause arterial intimal damage, creating tears that predispose to thrombosis.

Aggressive retraction or inadvertent inclusion of the femoral vein during vessel manipulation can result in venous laceration.

Additionally, failure to identify and protect small arterial branches such as the circumflex and epigastric arteries before vessel mobilization can lead to avulsion and bleeding.

Complications 

Lymphatic injury and leakage

Inadequate control of lymphatic vessels during dissection can result in persistent lymphatic drainage, leading to seroma formation and delayed wound healing. Identification and clipping of lymphatic vessels is recommended, if feasible, as dissection progresses through the subcutaneous layers.

Femoral vein injury

The femoral vein lies medial to the artery and can be injured during dissection, retraction, or improper instrument passage. Injury causes massive hemorrhage and is difficult to repair due to the thin vein wall.

Femoral nerve injury

Nerve branches lateral to the artery can be injured during dissection or if included in fascial closure sutures, causing motor weakness and sensory deficits. 

Circumflex vein injury

Veins crossing the deep femoral artery can cause significant bleeding if injured during dissection or vessel mobilization.

title: Fasciotomy of the thigh
secondary_keywords: compartment syndrome thigh fasciotomy; thigh compartment release procedure; lateral thigh fasciotomy steps; fasciotomy operative technique lower extremity; thigh decompression surgical approach
all_keywords: thigh fasciotomy technique; compartment syndrome thigh fasciotomy; thigh compartment release procedure; lateral thigh fasciotomy steps; fasciotomy operative technique lower extremity; thigh decompression surgical approach
source_knowledge_excerpt: Patient positioning, anesthesia and preparationLandmarks and lateral incision siteLateral incisionReleasing the anterior compartmentReleasing the posterior compartmentLandmarks and the medial incision siteMedial incisionReleasing the medial compartmentManagement and closure techniqueSurgeon: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon) Steps Patient positioning, anesthesia and preparation The patient is in a supine position, with the entire limb exposed.   The position should allow access to all compartments (anterior, posterior and medial) of the thigh.  General anesthesia is preferred since patients...
source_knowledge:
Patient positioning, anesthesia and preparationLandmarks and lateral incision siteLateral incisionReleasing the anterior compartmentReleasing the posterior compartmentLandmarks and the medial incision siteMedial incisionReleasing the medial compartmentManagement and closure techniqueSurgeon: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon)
Steps Patient positioning, anesthesia and preparation The patient is in a supine position, with the entire limb exposed. 

The position should allow access to all compartments (anterior, posterior and medial) of the thigh. 
General anesthesia is preferred since patients needing fasciotomy are usually critically ill. 
The use of a tourniquet should be avoided, as it further reduces blood flow to tissues that are already ischemic. Additionally, the tourniquet may impair the evaluation of tissue vitality. 

Landmarks and incision site The lateral skin incision should provide optimal access to the anterior and posterior muscle compartments for decompression while keeping vital structures safe. 
Palpate the greater trochanter and the lateral condyle in the distal femur. 

The incision extends in a straight line from the greater trochanter to the lateral condyle. Correct incision location allows access to the anterior and posterior compartments from the same incision.

Lateral incisionThe goal is to properly expose the fascia lata to enable access to intermuscular septum from where the anterior and posterior compartments can be decompressed safely. 
Incise the skin with a scalpel, perpendicular to the skin, as planned. There are no critical structures directly underneath the skin.

Use a scalpel or diathermy and advance through the subcutis down to the fascia. Coagulate bleeding vessels.

Use retractors to gain better visibility and to expose the fascia lata. 

Anterior compartment release The goal is to decompress the muscles in the anterior compartment by opening the fascia and allowing the muscles to expand. 
Incise the fascia lata with a scalpel or scissors over the entire length so that the muscle can expand properly.

Sometimes the fascia lata is so unforgiving that, in addition to the longitudinal incision, some transversal relaxing incisions can be made. 
In the distal part of the thigh, the vastus lateralis muscle can be elevated to gain access to the posterior compartment through the intermuscular septum.

Posterior compartment release The goal is to decompress the muscles in the posterior compartment by opening the fascia and allowing the muscles to expand. 
After releasing the anterior compartment, the posterior compartment can be accessed through the intermuscular septum using a scalpel or scissors.

Expand the incision as far proximally as there’s palpable pressure in the posterior compartment.

Landmarks and medial incision site In most cases, releasing the pressure of the anterior and the posterior compartments is enough, but sometimes there is also pressure in the medial compartment that needs to be addressed. The medial compartment of the thigh contains the gracilis muscle and the adductors. 
The medial skin incision should provide optimal access for decompression of the medial muscle compartment. 
The landmarks for the medial incision are the pubic bone and the medial condyle of the distal femur. 
Plan the incision between these landmarks.

Medial incision The goal is to expose the fascia and allow safe access to medial compartment for decompression. 
Incise the skin with a scalpel perpendicular to the skin, as planned. 

Advance through the subcutaneous tissue down to the fascia with a scalpel or scissors. Beware of the greater saphenous vein in the subcutis. Coagulate bleeding vessels. 

Use retractors to gain better visibility and for optimal exposure of the fascia. 

Medial compartment release The goal is to decompress the muscles in the medial compartment by opening the fascia and allowing the muscles to expand. 
Incise the fascia with a scalpel or scissors.

Depending on the tension in the compartment, extend the incision both proximally and distally. 

One incision of the medial fascia is sufficient to decompress the entire compartment. There’s no need to explore deeper in between the sartorius and gracilis muscles. 

Aftercare General guidelines Immediately after the operation: Highly absorbent dressings and bandage (not too tight). A silicone net or a paraffin gauze can be placed immediately on the wound to make dressing change more comfortable for the patient. 
Bedside evaluation of the wound the next day. Evaluation in the operating room for possible wound closure at 48-72 hours post-fasciotomy. Eventually, during closure, only the skin needs to be closed on the medial side. On the lateral side the fascia should also be closed to offer support for the muscles.

General 

Medical experts: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon)

Names of procedure: Fasciotomy of the thigh, thigh fasciotomy

Goal of operation 

To prevent or treat ischemic tissue injury by releasing pressure in the muscle compartments. 

Problem

Increased pressure in the muscle compartments of the thigh caused by trauma or severe disturbance. 

Diagnosis

Acute compartment syndrome (ICD-10: T79.6)

Short pathophysiological description 

Compartment syndrome occurs when the pressure within a closed muscle compartment increases to a point where it exceeds the perfusion pressure, leading to decreased blood flow to the compartment. Compartment syndrome can be caused by trauma (fracture, hemorrhage, burn), prolonged compression of the muscle (alcohol-related sleeping on the limb, prolonged compression of the limb), or infection. 

If left untreated the pressure will compromise the blood supply to the muscles and nerves and subsequently lead to ischemia and eventually necrosis. During fasciotomy the fascia surrounding the muscles is incised which allows the pressure to be released, restoring blood flow to the affected tissues, preventing further damage, and enabling healing. 

Key anatomical structures 

Compartments of the thigh

Anterior compartment 

Posterior compartment 

Medical compartment 

Fascia lata 

Intermuscular septum 

Great saphenous vein 

Pitfalls 

Short incisions 

If the incisions are not extended enough proximally and distally, they may not release all the pressure, leading to potential tissue damage. The fascia should be released over its entire length and the skin incision should be long enough to allow good visibility and not cause the expanding muscle to be trapped by the skin edges. 

Inadequate achievement of visibility 

Most complications are associated with inadequate visibility, or because the procedure has been attempted with an incision that does not extend far enough proximally and distally (i.e. “blindly”). This can lead to complications, i.e. muscle necrosis in the unopened compartment. 

To release the posterior compartment, a large retractor should be used, or the assistant should elevate the lateral vastus muscle to provide excellent visibility. 

Careless tissue handling

There are no major vascular structures in the lateral part of the thigh. A few major perforant branches may run through the fascia to the skin, but these can be managed by cauterization. 

Medially, avoid unnecessary contact with the great saphenous vein. If the vein is in the way or is accidentally severed, it should be ligated. Any long-term detriment to the patient is usually very minor.

Complications 

Muscle necrosis

The greatest problem in fasciotomy is failure to release the symptomatic compartment. The subsequent increase in pressure results in muscle necrosis and partial or total loss of the muscle, causing local functional deficit. 

Injury to the greater saphenous vein 

There is a risk of injury to the great saphenous vein from the medial incision. Unnecessary contact with the vein should be avoided, but in the case of injury or in need of better access to the surgical field the vein or its branches can be ligated.

title: Fasciotomy of the lower leg (two incision technique)
primary_keyword: lower leg fasciotomy two incision technique
secondary_keywords: four compartment fasciotomy leg steps; compartment syndrome leg fasciotomy procedure; two incision lower leg fasciotomy; leg fasciotomy surgical technique; lower extremity compartment release steps
all_keywords: lower leg fasciotomy two incision technique; four compartment fasciotomy leg steps; compartment syndrome leg fasciotomy procedure; two incision lower leg fasciotomy; leg fasciotomy surgical technique; lower extremity compartment release steps
source_knowledge_excerpt: Lateral landmarksAdvance until the crural fasciaIdentify the intermuscular septum and inciseRelease the anterior compartmentRelease the lateral compartmentMedial landmakrsSuperifical posterior compartment fasciaRelease the superficial posterior compartmentAdvance to the deep posterior compartmentDetach the medial soleus bridgeMake sure no constrictions remainsSurgeon: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon)  StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparation The patient is in supine position, allowing access to both the medial and lateral aspects of the lower leg.  Preferred form of anesthesia is...
source_knowledge:
Lateral landmarksAdvance until the crural fasciaIdentify the intermuscular septum and inciseRelease the anterior compartmentRelease the lateral compartmentMedial landmakrsSuperifical posterior compartment fasciaRelease the superficial posterior compartmentAdvance to the deep posterior compartmentDetach the medial soleus bridgeMake sure no constrictions remainsSurgeon: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon)

StepsPatient positioning, anesthesia and preparation The patient is in supine position, allowing access to both the medial and lateral aspects of the lower leg. 
Preferred form of anesthesia is general anesthesia. Spinal anesthesia may be used, but it may affect the postoperative monitoring of the patient. Local anesthesia is generally not sufficient for proper fasciotomies. 
A tourniquet should not be used, it will only aggravate ischemia-reperfusion injury to the tissues.
Planning your incisions:Verify the correct limb. Draw the landmarks on both sides before incisingMarking key landmarks will aid in correct placement of the incisions, particularly when the limb is swollen and/or deformed. The anterior tibial ridge is subcutaneous and always palpable even in a swollen limb. It serves as a useful and reliable midpoint between the two incisions. 

Lateral incision - Releasing the anterior and lateral compartmentsSkin incisionOn the lateral side, locate both ends of fibula and the fibular shaft. Mark them and your planned incision line on the skin before proceeding. 

The incision should be made about 1 fingerbreadth anterior to the front edge of fibula to later gain visibility to the intermuscular septum between anterior and lateral compartment. 

The incision should extend from about three fingerbreadths below the head of fibula caudally to about three fingerbreadths above the lateral malleolus.

Advancing through the subcutaneous fatUse scissors to dissect through the subcutaneous tissue until the crural fascia surface. 

You can use Adson retractors to retract the fat to improve visibility, if necessary. 

Exposing the fascia encasing the lateral and anterior compartmentsWhen necessary, raise skin flaps on both sides of the incision to adequately expose the fasciae. 

The superficial peroneal nerve usually runs through the crural fascia, near the intermuscular septum, and courses anteriorly. Look out for this nerve and protect it, if visible. The nerve may exit either the anterior or the peroneal compartment and, in some cases, appears as two separate branches: one coming from the anterior compartment and the other from the peroneal compartment. The nerve or its branches may run through the fascia at various levels. Therefore, look out for any nerve structures along the operative field, also distally, and avoid damaging them. 

Identifying the intermuscular septum between the compartmentsYou should now be able to identify both the anterior and lateral compartments and be able to separate them from one another by identifying the intermuscular septum. The septum appears as a strand of denser connective tissue between the compartments.

To better differentiate between the septum and compartmental fascia, you can make a horizontal incision over the anterior and lateral compartmental fascia. The septum should be more clearly visible and identifiable now. This is a crucial step, as a failure to separate between the two compartments could lead to one of them remaining unreleased and result in tissue damage. 

Fasciotomy of anterior and lateral compartmentAdvance to the fasciotomy, i.e. cutting open the fascia covering both compartments. Make the longitudinal incisions approximately 1cm away from the septum. Use curved scissors, preferably with their tips pointing away from the septum. Remember to look out for nerve structures, especially the superficial peroneal nerve. 

Make sure that your fasciotomies cover the entire length of the compartments. Where muscle tissue ends, the compartment ends as well. That is the adequate endpoint for your fasciotomy cuts. 

Quality controlAssess both compartments and make sure that they have been adequately released. No constricting fascial tissue or skin should be found at the end of the incision. 

Assess tissue vitality. Viable muscle has a healthy red/pink color, bleeds when cut and contracts when stimulated with cautery. Any necrotic tissue should be debrided. 

Once the lateral side is completed, you may advance to the medial incision for releasing the posterior compartments. 

Medial incision - Release of the superficial and deep posterior compartmentsSkin incisionOn the medial side, palpate and mark the posteromedial edge of tibia and the medial malleolus. 

The medial incision is made 1-2 fingerbreadths posterior to the palpable posteromedial edge of tibia. Through this incision the goal is to release the superficial posterior compartment and to be able to detach part of the soleus muscle (soleuos bridge) from the posterior surface of the tibia for the complete release of the deep posterior compartment.



The skin incision should extend from approximately three fingerbreadths below the tibial tuberosity caudally to just above the malleolus. Medial skin incision extends to the same level as lateral incision. 

Advance through the subcutaneous fat and expose the superficial posterior compartmentAdvance through the subcutaneous fat tissue in the same manner as on the lateral side. 

Look out for the saphenous nerve and the great saphenous vein - identify and preserve them. Ligate vein tributaries when necessary. 



Detach subcutaneous tissue from the compartmental fascia of the superficial posterior compartment. 

Incise the fasciaDistally, the muscles of the deep posterior compartment are not tightly attached to the posterior surface of tibia. Start your fasciotomy cut approximately at the distal third of your skin incision. You will advance through the superficial compartmental fascia to reach the deep posterior compartment. 

Once you have exposed the posterior tibial surface distally, you can proceed to full fasciotomy of the superficial compartment proximally with scissors. 

Releasing the deep posterior compartmentFor full release of the proximal part of the deep compartment, you will have to detach the distal portion of the soleus muscle's medial attachment (the soleus bridge) from the posterior aspect of tibia. This will reveal the proximal part of the deep compartmental fascia, and allow the release of it, again with scissors. 

Identification of the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle confirms the entry to the deep posterior compartment. Entry to the deep posterior compartment ensures the opening of the superficial posterior compartment. 



Quality controlAs on lateral side, assess both compartments and make sure that they have been adequately released. No constricting fascial tissue or skin should be found at the end of the incision.

Assess the viability of the muscles (color, consistency, contractility and perfusion). Debride any necrotic tissue.

HemostasisYou can now reassess the hemostasis and control minor hemorrhages with electrocautery, where necessary. 

Wound closure and aftercareGeneral guidelines:Generally, fasciotomy wounds are not closed at the time of the initial procedure, and the patient returns to the operating room every 24-72 hours for change of dressings, reevaluation of muscle viability, and gradual wound closure. Both the vessel-loop/shoelace technique and negative pressure wound dressings have been successfully used when the swelling persists and the closure is is not obtained at the first re-evaluation.

General

Medical experts: Ville Vänni (trauma surgeon), Mikael Åkerback (orthopedic surgeon)

Name of procedure: Two incision fasciotomy of the lower leg 

Goal of operation

To release the muscle compartments of the lower leg to prevent ischemic tissue damage, with the complete incision of their respective fasciae. 

Problem

Acute compartment syndrome 

Diagnostic

Acute compartment syndrome (ICD-10: T79.6)

Short pathophysiological description

The muscles of the lower leg are divided into four anatomical compartments, all surrounded by their respective fasciae. Acute compartment syndrome occurs when rapid pressure elevation within the compartments, for example after trauma, impairs the blood flow within the compartments. Symptoms may include severe pain, muscle tightness, paresthesia, and, ultimately, loss of sensation and muscle weakness/paralysis. If untreated, the condition may lead to ischemic tissue damage of the structures within the affected compartments. 

Key anatomical structures

The four muscle compartments of the lower leg 

Anterior compartment 

Tibialis anterior

Extensor hallucis longus

Extensor digitorum longus

Peroneus tertius

Lateral (peroneal) compartment 

Peroneus longus 

Peroneus brevis

Superficial posterior compartment 

Soleus 

Gastrocnemius 

Plantaris 

Deep posterior compartment 

Tibialis posterior 

Flexor digitorum longus 

Flexor hallucis longus 

Popliteus 

Crural fascia and the septa between compartments 

Superficial peroneal nerve 

Saphenous nerve

Great saphenous vein

Pitfalls

Incorrect site of lateral incision

The lateral incision should be anterior enough to gain access to the anterior compartment, otherwise, this access may prove challenging. The incision should be made about 1 fingerbreadth anterior to the front edge of fibula.

Disorientation during surgery

It is important to identify and detach the soleus muscle's medial attachment (the soleus bridge) from the posterior aspect of tibia. This will reveal the proximal part of the deep compartmental fascia and allow the release of it. 

Too short incisions

Both the skin and the fasciae should be cut open so that the compartments are released all the way. The muscles under their fasciae indicate the correct length of the cut, that is, when the muscle tissue ends, the cut is sufficient. 

Careless tissue handling

The superficial peroneal nerve runs close to the lateral incision and the saphenous nerve close to the medial one. Also, take into consideration the possibility of anatomical variations.

Complications

Not releasing the anterior compartment/muscle necrosis

Making the skin incision too posteriorly on the lateral side may result in not releasing the anterior compartment, and a consequent loss of dorsiflexion function of the ankle. 

Also, failing to identify the correct intermuscular septum between the anterior and lateral compartments during the lateral incision will most likely result in not releasing the anterior compartment. Remember that the anterior compartment is the easiest of the leg compartments but is also the compartment most commonly left unreleased. 



Not releasing the deep posterior compartment /muscle necrosis 

This results from improper recognition and/or visualization of the compartments. Make sure to detach the distal portion of the soleus bridge (the proximal attachment of soleus muscle to tibia and fibula) from tibia to fully expose and incise the deep posterior compartment. 

This also allows you to palpate the posterior surface of tibia and to possibly identify the neurovascular bundle. Identification of the deep vessels confirms the release of the deep compartment.

Superficial peroneal nerve injury 

Look out for the superficial peroneal nerve when advancing through the subcutaneous tissue and incising the fascia on the lateral side. It usually runs through the mid part of the crural fascia but may also present either more proximally or distally. Sometimes, the nerve has two parallel branches that both come through the fascia at varying levels. Damaging this nerve can result in inability to evert the foot and loss of sensation over the dorsum of the foot. 



Saphenous nerve injury 

The saphenous vein runs alongside with the greater saphenous vein. Identify and protect these structures when advancing through the subcutaneous tissue in medial incision. Damaging the saphenous nerve leads to loss of sensation in the medial aspect of the foot. 



Great saphenous vein injury 

During the medial incision, damaging the great saphenous vein is also possible. Avoid it by identifying it and coursing either anteriorly or posteriorly to it. However, if the vein is damaged it can be ligated with impunity.

title: Fasciotomy of the forearm
secondary_keywords: upper extremity compartment syndrome fasciotomy; volar forearm fasciotomy steps; forearm compartment release procedure; forearm decompression surgical technique; hand and forearm fasciotomy approach
